title: Quadratic Formula
text: Quadratic Formula. For ax^2 + bx + c = 0, the solutions are x = (-b ± sqrt(b^2 - 4ac)) / (2a). The discriminant D = b^2 - 4ac determines the nature of roots: D > 0 gives two distinct real roots, D = 0 gives one repeated root, D < 0 gives complex conjugate roots. Example: Solve x^2 - 5x + 6 = 0. Here a=1, b=-5, c=6. D = 25 - 24 = 1. x = (5 ± 1)/2, so x = 3 or x = 2.

title: Sum and Product of Roots
text: Sum and Product of Roots. For the quadratic equation ax^2 + bx + c = 0 with roots alpha and beta: sum of roots alpha + beta = -b/a, and product of roots alpha * beta = c/a. These relations are known as Vieta's formulas for quadratics and are fundamental in forming equations from given roots. Example: For 2x^2 - 7x + 3 = 0, sum of roots = 7/2, product of roots = 3/2. Verification: roots are 3 and 1/2, sum = 3.5 = 7/2, product = 1.5 = 3/2.

title: Formation of Quadratic Equation from Roots
text: Formation of Quadratic Equation from Roots. If alpha and beta are the desired roots, then the quadratic equation is x^2 - (alpha + beta)x + alpha*beta = 0, i.e., x^2 - (sum of roots)x + (product of roots) = 0. This is the reverse application of Vieta's formulas. Example: Form a quadratic equation whose roots are 3 and -2. Sum = 3 + (-2) = 1, Product = 3 * (-2) = -6. Equation: x^2 - x - 6 = 0.

title: Nature of Roots and Discriminant Analysis
text: Nature of Roots and Discriminant Analysis. For ax^2 + bx + c = 0 with a, b, c real: If D > 0 and a perfect square, roots are real, distinct, and rational. If D > 0 but not a perfect square, roots are real, distinct, and irrational (conjugate surds). If D = 0, roots are real and equal (each = -b/2a). If D < 0, roots are complex conjugates of the form p ± iq. For a, b, c rational and D > 0: irrational roots always occur in conjugate pairs. Example: For x^2 - 6x + 9 = 0, D = 36 - 36 = 0, so roots are equal: x = 3, 3. For x^2 + x + 1 = 0, D = 1 - 4 = -3 < 0, so roots are complex: x = (-1 ± i*sqrt(3))/2.

title: Condition for Common Roots
text: Condition for Common Roots. Two quadratic equations a1*x^2 + b1*x + c1 = 0 and a2*x^2 + b2*x + c2 = 0 have one common root if (a1*c2 - a2*c1)^2 = (a1*b2 - a2*b1)(b1*c2 - b2*c1). They have both roots common if a1/a2 = b1/b2 = c1/c2. Example: Equations x^2 - 5x + 6 = 0 and x^2 - 3x + 2 = 0 share the common root x = 2. Verify: (1*2 - 1*6)^2 = (-4)^2 = 16; (1*(-3) - 1*(-5))*(-5*2 - (-3)*6) = (2)(8) = 16. Condition satisfied.

title: Factor Theorem
formula_latex: f(a) = 0 \iff (x - a) \text{ is a factor of } f(x)
text: Factor Theorem. If f(x) is a polynomial and f(a) = 0, then (x - a) is a factor of f(x). Conversely, if (x - a) is a factor of f(x), then f(a) = 0. This theorem is essential for factoring higher-degree polynomials and finding their roots. Example: Is (x - 2) a factor of f(x) = x^3 - 6x^2 + 11x - 6? f(2) = 8 - 24 + 22 - 6 = 0. Yes, (x - 2) is a factor. Dividing: f(x) = (x - 2)(x^2 - 4x + 3) = (x - 1)(x - 2)(x - 3).

title: Remainder Theorem
text: Remainder Theorem. If a polynomial f(x) is divided by (x - a), then the remainder is f(a). More generally, when f(x) is divided by (ax - b), the remainder is f(b/a). This theorem provides a quick way to evaluate remainders without performing long division. Example: Find the remainder when f(x) = x^3 + 3x^2 - 2x + 1 is divided by (x - 1). Remainder = f(1) = 1 + 3 - 2 + 1 = 3.

title: Relationship Between Zeros and Coefficients of a Cubic Polynomial
text: Relationship Between Zeros and Coefficients of a Cubic Polynomial. For a cubic polynomial ax^3 + bx^2 + cx + d = 0 with roots alpha, beta, gamma: alpha + beta + gamma = -b/a, alpha*beta + beta*gamma + gamma*alpha = c/a, alpha*beta*gamma = -d/a. These are Vieta's formulas extended to degree 3. Example: For x^3 - 6x^2 + 11x - 6 = 0 with roots 1, 2, 3: sum = 6 = -(-6)/1, sum of products taken two at a time = 2 + 6 + 3 = 11 = 11/1, product = 6 = -(-6)/1.

title: Degree and Division Algorithm for Polynomials
text: Degree and Division Algorithm for Polynomials. The degree of a polynomial is the highest power of the variable with a non-zero coefficient. Division Algorithm: For polynomials f(x) and g(x) with deg(g) > 0, there exist unique polynomials q(x) and r(x) such that f(x) = g(x)*q(x) + r(x), where r(x) = 0 or deg(r) < deg(g). This is analogous to Euclid's division lemma for integers. Example: Divide f(x) = 2x^3 + 3x^2 - 5x + 1 by g(x) = x^2 + 1. Then q(x) = 2x + 3 and r(x) = -7x - 2. Verify: (x^2 + 1)(2x + 3) + (-7x - 2) = 2x^3 + 3x^2 + 2x + 3 - 7x - 2 = 2x^3 + 3x^2 - 5x + 1.

title: Nth Term of an Arithmetic Progression
text: Nth Term of an Arithmetic Progression. An Arithmetic Progression (AP) is a sequence where the difference between consecutive terms is constant. This constant difference is called the common difference (d). The nth term of an AP with first term a and common difference d is a_n = a + (n-1)d. Example: Find the 20th term of the AP: 3, 7, 11, 15, ... Here a = 3, d = 4. a_20 = 3 + (20 - 1) * 4 = 3 + 76 = 79.

title: Sum of First N Terms of an AP
text: Sum of First N Terms of an AP. The sum of the first n terms of an AP is S_n = n/2 * [2a + (n-1)d] or equivalently S_n = n/2 * (a + l), where a is the first term, d is the common difference, and l is the last (nth) term. The second form is useful when the last term is known. Example: Find the sum of the first 10 terms of the AP: 5, 8, 11, ... Here a = 5, d = 3, n = 10. S_10 = 10/2 * [2(5) + 9(3)] = 5 * [10 + 27] = 5 * 37 = 185.

title: Properties of Arithmetic Progressions
formula_latex: 2b = a + c \text{ (AM condition)}, \quad a_n = S_n - S_{n-1}
text: Properties of Arithmetic Progressions. Key properties: (1) If a, b, c are in AP, then 2b = a + c (b is the arithmetic mean). (2) If each term is increased/decreased by a constant, the resulting sequence is an AP with the same common difference. (3) If each term is multiplied/divided by a non-zero constant k, the resulting sequence is an AP with common difference kd or d/k. (4) Sum of terms equidistant from the beginning and end is constant and equals a + l. (5) The nth term can also be written as a_n = S_n - S_(n-1) for n >= 2. Example: Three numbers in AP have sum 27 and product 648. Let them be a-d, a, a+d. Sum: 3a = 27 => a = 9. Product: (9-d)(9)(9+d) = 648 => 9(81 - d^2) = 648 => d^2 = 9 => d = 3. Numbers: 6, 9, 12.

title: Nth Term of a Geometric Progression
text: Nth Term of a Geometric Progression. A Geometric Progression (GP) is a sequence where the ratio of consecutive terms is constant. This constant ratio is called the common ratio (r). The nth term of a GP with first term a and common ratio r is a_n = a * r^(n-1). Example: Find the 8th term of the GP: 2, 6, 18, 54, ... Here a = 2, r = 3. a_8 = 2 * 3^7 = 2 * 2187 = 4374.

title: Sum of First N Terms of a GP
text: Sum of First N Terms of a GP. The sum of the first n terms of a GP with first term a and common ratio r (r != 1) is S_n = a(r^n - 1)/(r - 1) when r > 1, or S_n = a(1 - r^n)/(1 - r) when r < 1. When r = 1, S_n = na. Example: Find the sum of first 5 terms of the GP: 3, 6, 12, 24, ... Here a = 3, r = 2, n = 5. S_5 = 3(2^5 - 1)/(2 - 1) = 3 * 31 / 1 = 93.

title: Sum of Infinite Geometric Series
text: Sum of Infinite Geometric Series. For an infinite GP with first term a and common ratio r where |r| < 1, the sum converges to S_infinity = a / (1 - r). If |r| >= 1, the infinite series diverges and has no finite sum. This formula is widely used in problems involving recurring decimals and limits. Example: Find the sum of the infinite GP: 1, 1/2, 1/4, 1/8, ... Here a = 1, r = 1/2. S = 1 / (1 - 1/2) = 1 / (1/2) = 2. Also: express 0.333... as a fraction. 0.333... = 3/10 + 3/100 + ... = (3/10)/(1 - 1/10) = (3/10)/(9/10) = 1/3.

title: Properties of Geometric Progressions
formula_latex: b^2 = ac \text{ (GM condition)}, \quad GM = (a_1 a_2 \cdots a_n)^{1/n}
text: Properties of Geometric Progressions. Key properties: (1) If a, b, c are in GP, then b^2 = ac (b is the geometric mean). (2) The product of terms equidistant from the beginning and end is constant: a_1 * a_n = a_2 * a_(n-1) = ... (3) If each term of a GP is raised to the same power, the result is also a GP. (4) The geometric mean of n positive numbers a_1, a_2, ..., a_n is (a_1 * a_2 * ... * a_n)^(1/n). (5) For any three consecutive terms, the middle term squared equals the product of the other two. Example: Three positive numbers in GP have sum 26 and product 216. Let them be a/r, a, ar. Product: a^3 = 216 => a = 6. Sum: 6/r + 6 + 6r = 26 => 6/r + 6r = 20 => 6r^2 - 20r + 6 = 0 => r = 3 or 1/3. Numbers: 2, 6, 18.

title: Fundamental Properties of Logarithms
formula_latex: \log_a(mn) = \log_a m + \log_a n, \quad \log_a\left(\frac{m}{n}\right) = \log_a m - \log_a n, \quad \log_a(m^k) = k\log_a m
text: Fundamental Properties of Logarithms. Logarithm is the inverse of exponentiation: log_a(x) = y means a^y = x, where a > 0, a != 1, x > 0. Key properties: (1) log_a(mn) = log_a(m) + log_a(n), (2) log_a(m/n) = log_a(m) - log_a(n), (3) log_a(m^k) = k * log_a(m), (4) log_a(a) = 1, (5) log_a(1) = 0, (6) a^(log_a(x)) = x. Example: Simplify log_2(32) + log_2(4) - log_2(8). = log_2(2^5) + log_2(2^2) - log_2(2^3) = 5 + 2 - 3 = 4. Alternatively: log_2(32 * 4 / 8) = log_2(16) = 4.

title: Change of Base Formula
text: Change of Base Formula. The change of base formula allows converting logarithms from one base to another: log_a(b) = log_c(b) / log_c(a) for any valid base c. Special cases: log_a(b) = 1/log_b(a), and log_a(b) * log_b(c) = log_a(c) (chain rule). Common bases are 10 (common log) and e (natural log, ln). Example: Find log_3(7) using common logarithms. log_3(7) = log(7)/log(3) = 0.8451/0.4771 = 1.7712 (approximately). Also verify: log_2(8) = log(8)/log(2) = 0.9031/0.3010 = 3, which matches since 2^3 = 8.

title: Logarithmic Equations and Inequalities
text: Logarithmic Equations and Inequalities. When solving logarithmic equations: (1) Always check the domain (argument > 0, base > 0 and != 1). (2) Use properties to combine or simplify logarithms. (3) Convert to exponential form when needed. For logarithmic inequalities, the direction of inequality reverses when the base is between 0 and 1: if 0 < a < 1, then log_a(x) > log_a(y) implies x < y. Example: Solve log_2(x - 1) + log_2(x + 1) = 3. => log_2((x-1)(x+1)) = 3 => x^2 - 1 = 8 => x^2 = 9 => x = ±3. Check domain: x - 1 > 0 and x + 1 > 0, so x > 1. Only x = 3 is valid.

title: Linear Inequalities
formula_latex: ax + b > 0 \implies x > -\frac{b}{a} \text{ if } a > 0, \quad x < -\frac{b}{a} \text{ if } a < 0
text: Linear Inequalities. A linear inequality in one variable has the form ax + b > 0 (or <, >=, <=). Rules: (1) Adding/subtracting the same number on both sides preserves the inequality. (2) Multiplying/dividing by a positive number preserves the inequality. (3) Multiplying/dividing by a negative number reverses the inequality. The solution set is an interval on the real number line. Example: Solve: 3x - 7 >= 2x + 5 and 2x + 3 < 15. From the first: x >= 12. From the second: x < 6. Intersection is empty, so no solution exists.

title: Quadratic Inequalities
formula_latex: ax^2 + bx + c > 0: \text{ if } a > 0, \text{ solution is } x < \alpha \text{ or } x > \beta \text{ (where } \alpha < \beta \text{)}
text: Quadratic Inequalities. To solve ax^2 + bx + c > 0 (or <, >=, <=): (1) Find the roots of ax^2 + bx + c = 0. (2) If a > 0, the parabola opens upward, so the expression is positive outside the roots and negative between them. (3) If a < 0, the parabola opens downward, so the expression is negative outside the roots and positive between them. Use a number line or sign diagram to determine the solution set. Example: Solve x^2 - 5x + 6 < 0. Roots: x = 2 and x = 3 (a = 1 > 0, parabola opens upward). The expression is negative between the roots. Solution: 2 < x < 3, i.e., x in (2, 3).

title: Modulus (Absolute Value) Inequalities
formula_latex: |f(x)| < a \iff -a < f(x) < a, \quad |f(x)| > a \iff f(x) < -a \text{ or } f(x) > a
text: Modulus (Absolute Value) Inequalities. Key results for modulus inequalities: (1) |x| < a (a > 0) means -a < x < a. (2) |x| > a (a > 0) means x < -a or x > a. (3) |x - c| < a means c - a < x < c + a (values within distance a of c). (4) Triangle inequality: |a + b| <= |a| + |b|, and ||a| - |b|| <= |a - b|. Example: Solve |2x - 3| <= 5. => -5 <= 2x - 3 <= 5 => -2 <= 2x <= 8 => -1 <= x <= 4. Solution: x in [-1, 4].

title: Wavy Curve Method (Method of Intervals)
text: Wavy Curve Method (Method of Intervals). The Wavy Curve Method is used to solve polynomial and rational inequalities. Steps: (1) Factor the expression and find all zeros of the numerator and denominator. (2) Mark these critical points on a number line. (3) Determine the sign of the expression in the rightmost interval. (4) Alternate signs at each simple root; sign does not change at repeated (even multiplicity) roots. (5) Read off the solution based on the required inequality. Example: Solve (x - 1)(x - 3)/(x + 2) > 0. Critical points: x = -2, 1, 3. Sign in (3, inf): positive. Alternate: (1,3) negative, (-2,1) positive, (-inf,-2) negative. Solution: x in (-2, 1) union (3, inf).

title: Binomial Theorem for Positive Integer Index
text: Binomial Theorem for Positive Integer Index. The Binomial Theorem states: (a + b)^n = sum from r=0 to n of C(n,r) * a^(n-r) * b^r, where C(n,r) = n! / (r!(n-r)!) is the binomial coefficient. The expansion has (n+1) terms. The (r+1)th term, called the general term, is T_(r+1) = C(n,r) * a^(n-r) * b^r. Example: Expand (x + 2)^4. = C(4,0)x^4 + C(4,1)x^3(2) + C(4,2)x^2(4) + C(4,3)x(8) + C(4,4)(16) = x^4 + 8x^3 + 24x^2 + 32x + 16.

title: General Term and Middle Term
text: General Term and Middle Term. In the expansion of (a + b)^n, the general term is T_(r+1) = C(n,r) * a^(n-r) * b^r (for r = 0, 1, ..., n). For the middle term: if n is even, the middle term is T_(n/2 + 1). If n is odd, there are two middle terms: T_((n+1)/2) and T_((n+3)/2). Example: Find the middle term of (x + 1/x)^6. Here n = 6 (even), middle term is T_4 (r = 3). T_4 = C(6,3) * x^3 * (1/x)^3 = 20 * x^3 * x^(-3) = 20.

title: Properties of Binomial Coefficients
text: Properties of Binomial Coefficients. Important properties: (1) C(n,0) + C(n,1) + ... + C(n,n) = 2^n (sum of all coefficients). (2) C(n,0) - C(n,1) + C(n,2) - ... = 0 (alternating sum). (3) C(n,r) = C(n, n-r) (symmetry). (4) C(n,r) + C(n, r-1) = C(n+1, r) (Pascal's identity). (5) C(n,0)^2 + C(n,1)^2 + ... + C(n,n)^2 = C(2n, n) (Vandermonde's identity special case). Example: Verify for n = 4: C(4,0) + C(4,1) + C(4,2) + C(4,3) + C(4,4) = 1 + 4 + 6 + 4 + 1 = 16 = 2^4. Also, C(4,0)^2 + C(4,1)^2 + C(4,2)^2 + C(4,3)^2 + C(4,4)^2 = 1 + 16 + 36 + 16 + 1 = 70 = C(8,4).

title: Finding a Specific Term in Binomial Expansion
text: Finding a Specific Term in Binomial Expansion. To find the term independent of x (constant term) or the term containing a specific power of x in expansions like (x^p + a/x^q)^n, use the general term T_(r+1) = C(n,r) * (x^p)^(n-r) * (a/x^q)^r = C(n,r) * a^r * x^(p(n-r) - qr). Set the power of x to the desired value and solve for r. Example: Find the term independent of x in (x^2 + 1/x)^9. Power of x in T_(r+1): 2(9-r) - r = 18 - 3r. For constant term, 18 - 3r = 0 => r = 6. T_7 = C(9,6) * 1^6 * x^0 = 84.

title: Algebra of Complex Numbers
formula_latex: z = a + bi, \quad \bar{z} = a - bi, \quad z \cdot \bar{z} = a^2 + b^2
text: Algebra of Complex Numbers. A complex number is of the form z = a + bi, where a is the real part (Re(z)), b is the imaginary part (Im(z)), and i = sqrt(-1) with i^2 = -1. Operations: (a+bi) + (c+di) = (a+c) + (b+d)i; (a+bi)(c+di) = (ac-bd) + (ad+bc)i. The conjugate of z = a + bi is z_bar = a - bi. Division: (a+bi)/(c+di) is rationalized by multiplying numerator and denominator by (c - di). Example: Compute (3 + 2i)(1 - 4i). = 3(1) + 3(-4i) + 2i(1) + 2i(-4i) = 3 - 12i + 2i - 8i^2 = 3 - 10i + 8 = 11 - 10i. Divide (3+2i)/(1+i): multiply by (1-i)/(1-i) = (3+2i)(1-i)/2 = (3-3i+2i-2i^2)/2 = (5-i)/2 = 5/2 - i/2.

title: Modulus and Argument of a Complex Number
text: Modulus and Argument of a Complex Number. For z = a + bi: the modulus is |z| = sqrt(a^2 + b^2), representing the distance from the origin in the Argand plane. The argument (angle) theta = arg(z) = arctan(b/a), adjusted for the correct quadrant. The principal argument lies in (-pi, pi]. Polar form: z = |z|(cos(theta) + i*sin(theta)) = |z| * e^(i*theta). Example: Find the modulus and argument of z = -1 + i. |z| = sqrt(1 + 1) = sqrt(2). Since z is in the second quadrant (a < 0, b > 0), arg(z) = pi - arctan(1/1) = pi - pi/4 = 3*pi/4. Polar form: z = sqrt(2) * e^(i*3*pi/4).

title: De Moivre's Theorem
text: De Moivre's Theorem. De Moivre's Theorem states: (cos(theta) + i*sin(theta))^n = cos(n*theta) + i*sin(n*theta) for any integer n. This is invaluable for computing powers and roots of complex numbers. For nth roots of unity: the n solutions of z^n = 1 are z_k = e^(2*pi*k*i/n) for k = 0, 1, ..., n-1. The nth roots of unity are equally spaced on the unit circle. Example: Find (1 + i)^8. First, 1+i = sqrt(2) * e^(i*pi/4). So (1+i)^8 = (sqrt(2))^8 * e^(i*8*pi/4) = 16 * e^(i*2*pi) = 16(cos(2*pi) + i*sin(2*pi)) = 16.

title: Properties of Conjugate and Modulus
text: Properties of Conjugate and Modulus. Key properties: (1) conjugate(z1 + z2) = conjugate(z1) + conjugate(z2). (2) conjugate(z1 * z2) = conjugate(z1) * conjugate(z2). (3) |z1 * z2| = |z1| * |z2|. (4) |z1/z2| = |z1|/|z2|. (5) z * conjugate(z) = |z|^2. (6) |z1 + z2|^2 = |z1|^2 + |z2|^2 + 2*Re(z1 * conjugate(z2)). (7) Triangle inequality: |z1 + z2| <= |z1| + |z2|, and ||z1| - |z2|| <= |z1 - z2|. Example: Let z1 = 3 + 4i, z2 = 1 - 2i. |z1| = 5, |z2| = sqrt(5). |z1 * z2| = |z1| * |z2| = 5*sqrt(5). Verify: z1*z2 = (3+4i)(1-2i) = 3 - 6i + 4i - 8i^2 = 11 - 2i. |11 - 2i| = sqrt(121 + 4) = sqrt(125) = 5*sqrt(5).

title: Types of Matrices and Basic Operations
text: Types of Matrices and Basic Operations. A matrix is a rectangular array of numbers arranged in rows and columns. Key types: Row matrix (1 row), Column matrix (1 column), Square matrix (rows = columns), Diagonal matrix (non-diagonal elements are zero), Scalar matrix (diagonal matrix with equal diagonal elements), Identity matrix I (diagonal elements are 1), Zero matrix (all elements are 0), Symmetric matrix (A^T = A), Skew-symmetric matrix (A^T = -A). Operations: matrices of the same order can be added/subtracted element-wise; scalar multiplication multiplies each element. Example: Let A = [[1,2],[3,4]], B = [[5,6],[7,8]]. A + B = [[6,8],[10,12]]. 2A = [[2,4],[6,8]]. A^T = [[1,3],[2,4]]. A is neither symmetric nor skew-symmetric since A^T != A and A^T != -A.

title: Matrix Multiplication
text: Matrix Multiplication. For matrices A (m x n) and B (n x p), the product AB is an m x p matrix where (AB)_ij = sum of a_ik * b_kj for k = 1 to n. Matrix multiplication is not commutative in general: AB != BA. However, it is associative: (AB)C = A(BC), and distributive: A(B+C) = AB + AC. The identity matrix I satisfies AI = IA = A. Example: A = [[1,2],[3,4]], B = [[2,0],[1,3]]. AB = [[1*2+2*1, 1*0+2*3],[3*2+4*1, 3*0+4*3]] = [[4,6],[10,12]]. BA = [[2*1+0*3, 2*2+0*4],[1*1+3*3, 1*2+3*4]] = [[2,4],[10,14]]. Note AB != BA.

title: Determinant of a Matrix
formula_latex: \det\begin{pmatrix} a & b \\ c & d \end{pmatrix} = ad - bc
text: Determinant of a Matrix. The determinant is a scalar value computed from a square matrix. For a 2x2 matrix [[a,b],[c,d]], det = ad - bc. For a 3x3 matrix, expand along any row or column using cofactors. Properties: (1) det(A^T) = det(A). (2) Swapping two rows/columns changes the sign. (3) If two rows/columns are identical, det = 0. (4) det(kA) = k^n * det(A) for an n x n matrix. (5) det(AB) = det(A) * det(B). A matrix is singular if det = 0. Example: Find det([[2,3,1],[4,1,2],[1,5,3]]). Expanding along row 1: 2(1*3 - 2*5) - 3(4*3 - 2*1) + 1(4*5 - 1*1) = 2(3-10) - 3(12-2) + 1(20-1) = 2(-7) - 3(10) + 19 = -14 - 30 + 19 = -25.

title: Inverse of a Matrix
text: Inverse of a Matrix. A square matrix A is invertible (non-singular) if det(A) != 0. The inverse A^(-1) satisfies A * A^(-1) = A^(-1) * A = I. For a 2x2 matrix [[a,b],[c,d]], A^(-1) = (1/det(A)) * [[d,-b],[-c,a]]. For larger matrices, A^(-1) = (1/det(A)) * adj(A), where adj(A) is the adjugate (transpose of the cofactor matrix). Properties: (AB)^(-1) = B^(-1) * A^(-1). Example: Find the inverse of A = [[2,1],[5,3]]. det(A) = 6 - 5 = 1. A^(-1) = (1/1) * [[3,-1],[-5,2]] = [[3,-1],[-5,2]]. Verify: A * A^(-1) = [[2*3+1*(-5), 2*(-1)+1*2],[5*3+3*(-5), 5*(-1)+3*2]] = [[1,0],[0,1]] = I.

title: Cramer's Rule for Solving Linear Systems
text: Cramer's Rule for Solving Linear Systems. Cramer's Rule solves a system of n linear equations in n unknowns using determinants. For the system AX = B where A is the coefficient matrix, x_i = D_i / D, where D = det(A) != 0 and D_i is the determinant obtained by replacing the ith column of A with B. If D = 0 and all D_i = 0, the system may have infinitely many solutions. If D = 0 and some D_i != 0, the system is inconsistent. Example: Solve: 2x + y = 5, 3x - 2y = 4. D = det([[2,1],[3,-2]]) = -4-3 = -7. D_x = det([[5,1],[4,-2]]) = -10-4 = -14. D_y = det([[2,5],[3,4]]) = 8-15 = -7. x = -14/-7 = 2, y = -7/-7 = 1.

title: Set Operations
formula_latex: n(A \cup B) = n(A) + n(B) - n(A \cap B), \quad (A \cup B)' = A' \cap B'
text: Set Operations. A set is a well-defined collection of distinct objects. Key operations: Union (A ∪ B) contains all elements in A or B or both. Intersection (A ∩ B) contains elements in both A and B. Difference (A - B) contains elements in A but not in B. Complement (A') contains elements in the universal set U but not in A. Important formulas: n(A ∪ B) = n(A) + n(B) - n(A ∩ B). De Morgan's Laws: (A ∪ B)' = A' ∩ B' and (A ∩ B)' = A' ∪ B'. Example: Let U = {1,...,10}, A = {1,2,3,4,5}, B = {3,4,5,6,7}. A ∪ B = {1,2,3,4,5,6,7}. A ∩ B = {3,4,5}. A - B = {1,2}. A' = {6,7,8,9,10}. n(A ∪ B) = 5 + 5 - 3 = 7. Verify De Morgan: (A ∪ B)' = {8,9,10} = A' ∩ B' = {6,7,8,9,10} ∩ {1,2,8,9,10} = {8,9,10}.

title: Types of Relations
text: Types of Relations. A relation R from set A to set B is a subset of A x B. Types of relations on a set A: (1) Reflexive: (a, a) in R for all a in A. (2) Symmetric: (a, b) in R implies (b, a) in R. (3) Transitive: (a, b) in R and (b, c) in R implies (a, c) in R. (4) Equivalence relation: reflexive, symmetric, and transitive. An equivalence relation partitions the set into disjoint equivalence classes. Example: On Z (integers), define R by aRb iff a - b is divisible by 3. Reflexive: a - a = 0, divisible by 3. Symmetric: if 3 | (a-b), then 3 | (b-a). Transitive: if 3 | (a-b) and 3 | (b-c), then 3 | ((a-b)+(b-c)) = 3 | (a-c). So R is an equivalence relation with equivalence classes [0] = {...,-3,0,3,6,...}, [1] = {...,-2,1,4,7,...}, [2] = {...,-1,2,5,8,...}.

title: Types of Functions
text: Types of Functions. A function f: A -> B assigns each element of A to exactly one element of B. A is the domain, B is the codomain, and f(A) is the range. Types: (1) One-one (injective): f(a1) = f(a2) implies a1 = a2. (2) Onto (surjective): range = codomain, i.e., every element of B has a preimage. (3) Bijective: both one-one and onto (has an inverse function). (4) Composition: (f o g)(x) = f(g(x)). The number of functions from A to B (with |A|=m, |B|=n) is n^m; the number of one-one functions is n!/(n-m)! when n >= m. Example: Let f: R -> R defined by f(x) = 2x + 3. One-one: if 2x1+3 = 2x2+3, then x1 = x2. Onto: for any y in R, x = (y-3)/2 exists in R with f(x) = y. So f is bijective. Inverse: f^(-1)(y) = (y-3)/2.

title: Cartesian Product and Number of Relations
formula_latex: A \times B = \{(a, b) : a \in A, b \in B\}, \quad |A \times B| = |A| \cdot |B|
text: Cartesian Product and Number of Relations. The Cartesian product of sets A and B is A x B = {(a, b) : a in A, b in B}. If |A| = m and |B| = n, then |A x B| = mn. A relation from A to B is any subset of A x B, so the total number of relations from A to B is 2^(mn). The number of reflexive relations on a set of n elements is 2^(n^2 - n). The number of symmetric relations is 2^(n(n+1)/2). Example: A = {1, 2}, B = {a, b, c}. A x B = {(1,a),(1,b),(1,c),(2,a),(2,b),(2,c)}. |A x B| = 6. Total relations from A to B = 2^6 = 64. For a set C = {1,2,3}, reflexive relations on C: 2^(9-3) = 2^6 = 64.

title: Location of Roots of a Quadratic
text: Location of Roots of a Quadratic. For f(x) = ax^2 + bx + c (a > 0) with roots alpha and beta: (1) Both roots greater than k: D >= 0, f(k) > 0, and -b/(2a) > k. (2) Both roots less than k: D >= 0, f(k) > 0, and -b/(2a) < k. (3) k lies between the roots: f(k) < 0. (4) Both roots between k1 and k2: D >= 0, f(k1) > 0, f(k2) > 0, and k1 < -b/(2a) < k2. These conditions are crucial for JEE problems on range of parameters. Example: Find values of k such that both roots of x^2 - 6x + k = 0 are greater than 2. D >= 0: 36 - 4k >= 0, so k <= 9. f(2) > 0: 4 - 12 + k > 0, so k > 8. Vertex: -b/2a = 3 > 2 (satisfied). Answer: k in (8, 9].

title: Logarithmic Series and Special Identities
text: Logarithmic Series and Special Identities. Important identities: (1) log_a(b) * log_b(a) = 1. (2) log_a(b) * log_b(c) * log_c(a) = 1. (3) If a^x = b^y = c^z and b^2 = ac, then 1/x + 1/z = 2/y. (4) log_a(a^n) = n. (5) a^(log_a(N)) = N. These identities are frequently tested in competitive exams and require a solid understanding of the definition and change of base formula. Example: If log_2(3) = a and log_3(5) = b, find log_2(5). Using chain rule: log_2(5) = log_2(3) * log_3(5) = a * b = ab. Also: log_2(15) = log_2(3 * 5) = log_2(3) + log_2(5) = a + ab = a(1 + b).

title: Multinomial Theorem and Applications
formula_latex: (x_1 + x_2 + \cdots + x_k)^n = \sum \frac{n!}{n_1! n_2! \cdots n_k!} x_1^{n_1} x_2^{n_2} \cdots x_k^{n_k}
text: Multinomial Theorem and Applications. The multinomial theorem generalizes the binomial theorem: (x1 + x2 + ... + xk)^n = sum of [n! / (n1! * n2! * ... * nk!)] * x1^n1 * x2^n2 * ... * xk^nk, summed over all non-negative integers n1, n2, ..., nk with n1 + n2 + ... + nk = n. The number of terms in the expansion of (x1 + x2 + ... + xk)^n is C(n+k-1, k-1). Example: Find the coefficient of x^2*y*z in (x + y + z)^4. Here n1=2, n2=1, n3=1, n=4. Coefficient = 4! / (2! * 1! * 1!) = 24 / 2 = 12.

title: Geometric Interpretation of Complex Number Operations
text: Geometric Interpretation of Complex Number Operations. In the Argand plane, addition of complex numbers follows the parallelogram law. Multiplication by z2 = r*e^(i*theta) scales by r and rotates by theta. |z1 - z2| represents the distance between z1 and z2. The equation |z - z0| = r represents a circle centered at z0 with radius r. |z - z1| = |z - z2| represents the perpendicular bisector of the segment joining z1 and z2. arg((z - z1)/(z - z2)) = theta represents an arc of a circle through z1 and z2. Example: Find the locus of z such that |z - 2 - 3i| = 4. This is a circle centered at (2, 3) with radius 4: (x-2)^2 + (y-3)^2 = 16. Find locus of |z - 1| = |z + i|: perpendicular bisector of segment from (1,0) to (0,-1), which is the line x + y = 0 (after simplification from x^2 - 2x + 1 + y^2 = x^2 + y^2 + 2y + 1).

title: Arithmetic Mean and Insertion of Means
formula_latex: AM = \frac{a + b}{2}, \quad d = \frac{b - a}{k + 1}, \quad AM \geq GM
text: Arithmetic Mean and Insertion of Means. The Arithmetic Mean (AM) of n numbers a1, a2, ..., an is (a1 + a2 + ... + an)/n. To insert k arithmetic means between two numbers a and b: the common difference d = (b - a)/(k + 1), and the means are a + d, a + 2d, ..., a + kd. AM-GM Inequality: For positive reals, AM >= GM, i.e., (a + b)/2 >= sqrt(ab), with equality iff a = b. Example: Insert 4 arithmetic means between 3 and 18. d = (18 - 3)/(4 + 1) = 15/5 = 3. Means: 6, 9, 12, 15. Verify: 3, 6, 9, 12, 15, 18 is an AP with d = 3. AM-GM: For a = 4, b = 9: AM = 6.5, GM = 6. Indeed 6.5 >= 6.

title: Limit Definition (Epsilon-Delta)
text: Limit Definition (Epsilon-Delta). The limit of f(x) as x approaches a is L if for every epsilon > 0 there exists delta > 0 such that |f(x) - L| < epsilon whenever 0 < |x - a| < delta. This rigorous definition formalizes the intuitive notion that f(x) gets arbitrarily close to L as x gets close to a. Example: Find lim(x->2) (x^2 - 4)/(x - 2). Factor the numerator: (x+2)(x-2)/(x-2). Cancel (x-2) to get (x+2). Substitute x=2: 2+2 = 4. So the limit is 4.

title: L'Hopital's Rule
text: L'Hopital's Rule. L'Hopital's Rule states that if lim f(x)/g(x) gives an indeterminate form 0/0 or infinity/infinity, then the limit equals lim f'(x)/g'(x), provided the latter limit exists. The rule can be applied repeatedly if the result is still indeterminate. Example: Find lim(x->0) sin(x)/x. This is 0/0 form. Apply L'Hopital: lim(x->0) cos(x)/1 = cos(0) = 1.

title: Standard Limit: sin(x)/x
text: Standard Limit: sin(x)/x. The limit of sin(x)/x as x approaches 0 equals 1. This is one of the most fundamental standard limits in calculus. It is proved using the Squeeze Theorem with the geometric inequality sin(x) < x < tan(x) for 0 < x < pi/2. Example: Find lim(x->0) sin(5x)/(3x). Rewrite as (5/3) * sin(5x)/(5x). As x->0, 5x->0, so sin(5x)/(5x)->1. Answer: 5/3.

title: Standard Limit: (1 + 1/n)^n and Exponential Limits
text: Standard Limit: (1 + 1/n)^n and Exponential Limits. The limit of (1 + 1/n)^n as n approaches infinity equals e (Euler's number, approximately 2.71828). A useful generalization is lim(x->0) (1 + x)^(1/x) = e. These limits appear frequently in compound interest, growth models, and JEE problems. Example: Find lim(x->0) (e^(3x) - 1)/x. Rewrite as 3 * (e^(3x) - 1)/(3x). Let t = 3x; as x->0, t->0. So lim = 3 * 1 = 3.

title: Squeeze Theorem (Sandwich Theorem)
formula_latex: g(x) \leq f(x) \leq h(x) \text{ and } \lim_{x \to a} g(x) = \lim_{x \to a} h(x) = L \implies \lim_{x \to a} f(x) = L
text: Squeeze Theorem (Sandwich Theorem). If g(x) <= f(x) <= h(x) for all x near a (except possibly at a), and lim g(x) = lim h(x) = L as x->a, then lim f(x) = L. This theorem is essential when direct evaluation is difficult but bounding functions are available. Example: Find lim(x->0) x^2 * sin(1/x). Since -1 <= sin(1/x) <= 1, we have -x^2 <= x^2 sin(1/x) <= x^2. Both -x^2 and x^2 tend to 0 as x->0. By Squeeze Theorem, the limit is 0.

title: Limits at Infinity and Horizontal Asymptotes
formula_latex: \lim_{x \to \infty} \frac{a_n x^n + \cdots}{b_m x^m + \cdots} = \begin{cases} 0 & n < m \\ \frac{a_n}{b_m} & n = m \\ \pm\infty & n > m \end{cases}
text: Limits at Infinity and Horizontal Asymptotes. To evaluate limits as x approaches infinity for rational functions, divide numerator and denominator by the highest power of x. If degree of numerator < denominator, limit is 0. If degrees are equal, limit is the ratio of leading coefficients. If degree of numerator > denominator, the limit is infinity. Example: Find lim(x->infinity) (3x^2 + 5x)/(7x^2 - 2). Divide by x^2: (3 + 5/x)/(7 - 2/x^2). As x->infinity, 5/x->0 and 2/x^2->0. Limit = 3/7.

title: Standard Limit: log(1+x)/x
text: Standard Limit: log(1+x)/x. The limit of ln(1+x)/x as x approaches 0 is 1. More generally, lim(x->0) log_a(1+x)/x = 1/ln(a). This standard limit is frequently used in JEE problems involving logarithmic expressions. Example: Find lim(x->0) ln(1+3x)/(2x). Rewrite as (3/2) * ln(1+3x)/(3x). Let t = 3x; as x->0, t->0, and ln(1+t)/t -> 1. Answer: 3/2.

title: Definition of Continuity at a Point
formula_latex: f \text{ is continuous at } x = a \iff \lim_{x \to a} f(x) = f(a)
text: Definition of Continuity at a Point. A function f(x) is continuous at x = a if three conditions hold: (1) f(a) is defined, (2) lim(x->a) f(x) exists, and (3) lim(x->a) f(x) = f(a). If any condition fails, f is discontinuous at a. All polynomial functions are continuous everywhere. Example: Check continuity of f(x) = (x^2 - 1)/(x - 1) at x = 1. f(1) is undefined (0/0), so condition (1) fails. f is discontinuous at x = 1. However, lim(x->1) f(x) = lim(x->1) (x+1) = 2 exists, so it is a removable discontinuity.

title: Types of Discontinuity
formula_latex: \text{Jump: } \lim_{x \to a^-} f(x) \neq \lim_{x \to a^+} f(x), \quad \text{Removable: } \lim_{x \to a} f(x) \neq f(a)
text: Types of Discontinuity. Discontinuities are classified as: (1) Removable discontinuity: limit exists but does not equal f(a) or f(a) is undefined. (2) Jump discontinuity: left-hand and right-hand limits exist but are not equal. (3) Infinite discontinuity: at least one of the one-sided limits is infinite. (4) Oscillatory discontinuity: the function oscillates without settling (e.g., sin(1/x) at x=0). Example: f(x) = |x|/x has a jump discontinuity at x = 0. Left-hand limit = -1, right-hand limit = +1. Since -1 != 1, the limit does not exist and the discontinuity is a jump.

title: Intermediate Value Theorem (IVT)
formula_latex: f \text{ continuous on } [a,b],\ f(a) < k < f(b) \implies \exists\, c \in (a,b) : f(c) = k
text: Intermediate Value Theorem (IVT). If f is continuous on [a, b] and k is any value between f(a) and f(b), then there exists at least one c in (a, b) such that f(c) = k. A key corollary: if f(a) and f(b) have opposite signs, there is at least one root in (a, b). IVT is used to prove existence of roots. Example: Show x^3 - x - 1 = 0 has a root in (1, 2). Let f(x) = x^3 - x - 1. f(1) = 1 - 1 - 1 = -1 < 0. f(2) = 8 - 2 - 1 = 5 > 0. Since f is continuous and changes sign, by IVT there is a root c in (1, 2).

title: Derivative from First Principles
text: Derivative from First Principles. The derivative of f(x) at x = a is defined as the limit of the difference quotient: f'(a) = lim(h->0) [f(a+h) - f(a)]/h, provided this limit exists. Geometrically, it represents the slope of the tangent line at (a, f(a)). This is also called differentiation from first principles. Example: Find d/dx(x^2) from first principles. f'(x) = lim(h->0) [(x+h)^2 - x^2]/h = lim(h->0) [2xh + h^2]/h = lim(h->0) (2x + h) = 2x.

title: Power Rule for Differentiation
text: Power Rule for Differentiation. If f(x) = x^n where n is any real number, then f'(x) = n*x^(n-1). This rule extends to negative and fractional exponents. Combined with the constant multiple rule, d/dx[c*x^n] = c*n*x^(n-1). Example: Find d/dx(x^5 - 3x^3 + 7x). Using power rule term by term: 5x^4 - 9x^2 + 7. For fractional exponents: d/dx(sqrt(x)) = d/dx(x^(1/2)) = (1/2)x^(-1/2) = 1/(2*sqrt(x)).

title: Chain Rule
text: Chain Rule. If y = f(g(x)), then dy/dx = f'(g(x)) * g'(x). In Leibniz notation, if y = f(u) and u = g(x), then dy/dx = (dy/du)*(du/dx). The chain rule is essential for differentiating composite functions. Example: Find d/dx[sin(3x^2)]. Let u = 3x^2, so d/dx[sin(u)] = cos(u) * du/dx = cos(3x^2) * 6x = 6x*cos(3x^2).

title: Product Rule
text: Product Rule. If u(x) and v(x) are differentiable, then d/dx[u*v] = u'*v + u*v'. In words: derivative of first times second plus first times derivative of second. This extends to three functions: (uvw)' = u'vw + uv'w + uvw'. Example: Find d/dx[x^2 * sin(x)]. Let u = x^2, v = sin(x). u' = 2x, v' = cos(x). d/dx = 2x*sin(x) + x^2*cos(x).

title: Quotient Rule
text: Quotient Rule. If u(x) and v(x) are differentiable and v(x) != 0, then d/dx[u/v] = (u'v - uv')/v^2. A mnemonic: 'low d-high minus high d-low, over the square of what's below.' Example: Find d/dx[tan(x)] = d/dx[sin(x)/cos(x)]. u = sin(x), v = cos(x), u' = cos(x), v' = -sin(x). d/dx = [cos(x)*cos(x) - sin(x)*(-sin(x))]/cos^2(x) = [cos^2(x) + sin^2(x)]/cos^2(x) = 1/cos^2(x) = sec^2(x).

title: Derivatives of Trigonometric Functions
formula_latex: \frac{d}{dx}\sin x = \cos x, \quad \frac{d}{dx}\cos x = -\sin x, \quad \frac{d}{dx}\tan x = \sec^2 x
text: Derivatives of Trigonometric Functions. The standard trigonometric derivatives are: d/dx[sin(x)] = cos(x), d/dx[cos(x)] = -sin(x), d/dx[tan(x)] = sec^2(x), d/dx[cot(x)] = -csc^2(x), d/dx[sec(x)] = sec(x)tan(x), d/dx[csc(x)] = -csc(x)cot(x). These are frequently combined with the chain rule in JEE problems. Example: Find d/dx[sec(3x)]. Using chain rule: sec(3x)*tan(3x) * d/dx(3x) = 3*sec(3x)*tan(3x).

title: Implicit Differentiation
text: Implicit Differentiation. When y is defined implicitly by an equation F(x, y) = 0, we differentiate both sides with respect to x, treating y as a function of x and applying the chain rule. Every time we differentiate a term involving y, we multiply by dy/dx. Then solve for dy/dx. Example: Find dy/dx for x^2 + y^2 = 25. Differentiate: 2x + 2y*(dy/dx) = 0. Solve: dy/dx = -x/y. At (3, 4): dy/dx = -3/4.

title: Logarithmic Differentiation
formula_latex: y = f(x)^{g(x)} \implies \ln y = g(x) \ln f(x) \implies \frac{1}{y}\frac{dy}{dx} = g'(x)\ln f(x) + g(x)\frac{f'(x)}{f(x)}
text: Logarithmic Differentiation. For functions of the form y = [f(x)]^g(x) or products/quotients of many terms, take the natural log of both sides, differentiate using properties of logarithms, and solve for dy/dx. This technique simplifies complex expressions by converting products to sums and powers to products. Example: Find dy/dx for y = x^x. Take ln: ln(y) = x*ln(x). Differentiate: (1/y)*(dy/dx) = ln(x) + 1. So dy/dx = x^x * (ln(x) + 1).

title: Equation of Tangent and Normal Lines
formula_latex: \text{Tangent: } y - y_0 = f'(x_0)(x - x_0), \quad \text{Normal: } y - y_0 = -\frac{1}{f'(x_0)}(x - x_0)
text: Equation of Tangent and Normal Lines. At a point (x0, y0) on the curve y = f(x), the tangent line has slope m = f'(x0) and equation y - y0 = m(x - x0). The normal line is perpendicular to the tangent with slope -1/m and equation y - y0 = (-1/m)(x - x0). Example: Find the tangent to y = x^3 at (1, 1). f'(x) = 3x^2, f'(1) = 3. Tangent: y - 1 = 3(x - 1), i.e., y = 3x - 2. Normal: y - 1 = (-1/3)(x - 1), i.e., y = -x/3 + 4/3.

title: Maxima and Minima (First Derivative Test)
formula_latex: f'(c) = 0 \text{ and } f' \text{ changes } + \to - \implies \text{local max}; \quad - \to + \implies \text{local min}
text: Maxima and Minima (First Derivative Test). A function f(x) has a local maximum at x = c if f'(c) = 0 and f' changes sign from positive to negative at c. It has a local minimum if f' changes from negative to positive. Points where f'(c) = 0 or f'(c) is undefined are called critical points. Example: Find extrema of f(x) = x^3 - 3x + 2. f'(x) = 3x^2 - 3 = 3(x-1)(x+1) = 0 gives x = 1, -1. f' changes +to- at x=-1 (local max, f(-1)=4) and -to+ at x=1 (local min, f(1)=0).

title: Second Derivative Test for Extrema
formula_latex: f'(c) = 0 \text{ and } f''(c) > 0 \implies \text{local min}; \quad f''(c) < 0 \implies \text{local max}
text: Second Derivative Test for Extrema. If f'(c) = 0 and f''(c) > 0, then f has a local minimum at c. If f'(c) = 0 and f''(c) < 0, then f has a local maximum at c. If f''(c) = 0, the test is inconclusive and we must use the first derivative test or higher-order derivatives. Example: Find extrema of f(x) = x^4 - 4x^3. f'(x) = 4x^3 - 12x^2 = 4x^2(x - 3) = 0 gives x = 0, 3. f''(x) = 12x^2 - 24x. f''(0) = 0 (inconclusive), f''(3) = 108 - 72 = 36 > 0 (local min at x = 3, f(3) = -27).

title: Rate of Change
text: Rate of Change. The derivative f'(x) represents the instantaneous rate of change of f with respect to x. In related rates problems, if two quantities are related by an equation and both change with time t, we differentiate the equation with respect to t to find how their rates are related. Example: A circle's radius increases at 2 cm/s. Find the rate of area increase when r = 5 cm. A = pi*r^2. dA/dt = 2*pi*r*(dr/dt) = 2*pi*5*2 = 20*pi cm^2/s.

title: Rolle's Theorem
text: Rolle's Theorem. If f is continuous on [a, b], differentiable on (a, b), and f(a) = f(b), then there exists at least one c in (a, b) such that f'(c) = 0. Geometrically, there is a point where the tangent is horizontal. Rolle's theorem is a special case of the Mean Value Theorem. Example: Verify Rolle's theorem for f(x) = x^2 - 4x + 3 on [1, 3]. f(1) = 1 - 4 + 3 = 0, f(3) = 9 - 12 + 3 = 0. So f(1) = f(3). f'(x) = 2x - 4 = 0 gives x = 2 in (1, 3). Verified.

title: Lagrange's Mean Value Theorem (LMVT)
formula_latex: f'(c) = \frac{f(b) - f(a)}{b - a} \quad \text{for some } c \in (a, b)
text: Lagrange's Mean Value Theorem (LMVT). If f is continuous on [a, b] and differentiable on (a, b), then there exists at least one c in (a, b) such that f'(c) = [f(b) - f(a)]/(b - a). This means the instantaneous rate of change at some point equals the average rate of change over the interval. Example: Verify LMVT for f(x) = x^2 on [1, 3]. f(3) - f(1) = 9 - 1 = 8, b - a = 2, so RHS = 4. f'(x) = 2x = 4 gives x = 2 in (1, 3). Verified.

title: Increasing and Decreasing Functions
formula_latex: f'(x) > 0 \implies f \text{ is increasing}; \quad f'(x) < 0 \implies f \text{ is decreasing}
text: Increasing and Decreasing Functions. A function f is increasing on an interval if f'(x) > 0 for all x in that interval, and decreasing if f'(x) < 0. To find intervals of increase/decrease, solve f'(x) = 0 to find critical points, then test the sign of f' in each sub-interval. Example: Find where f(x) = x^3 - 6x^2 + 9x + 1 is increasing. f'(x) = 3x^2 - 12x + 9 = 3(x - 1)(x - 3). f'(x) > 0 when x < 1 or x > 3. So f is increasing on (-infinity, 1) and (3, infinity).

title: Antiderivative and Indefinite Integral
formula_latex: \int f(x)\, dx = F(x) + C \quad \text{where } F'(x) = f(x)
text: Antiderivative and Indefinite Integral. An antiderivative of f(x) is a function F(x) such that F'(x) = f(x). The indefinite integral of f(x) is the family of all antiderivatives: integral of f(x) dx = F(x) + C, where C is the constant of integration. The constant C accounts for all functions with the same derivative. Example: Find integral of (3x^2 + 2x) dx. Antiderivative of 3x^2 is x^3, antiderivative of 2x is x^2. Answer: x^3 + x^2 + C.

title: Power Rule for Integration
text: Power Rule for Integration. The integral of x^n dx = x^(n+1)/(n+1) + C for n != -1. When n = -1, integral of x^(-1) dx = integral of 1/x dx = ln|x| + C. This is the reverse of the power rule for differentiation. Example: Find integral of (x^4 + 1/x^2) dx = integral of (x^4 + x^(-2)) dx = x^5/5 + x^(-1)/(-1) + C = x^5/5 - 1/x + C.

title: Standard Integrals of Trigonometric and Exponential Functions
formula_latex: \int \sin x\, dx = -\cos x + C, \quad \int e^x\, dx = e^x + C, \quad \int \sec^2 x\, dx = \tan x + C
text: Standard Integrals of Trigonometric and Exponential Functions. Key standard integrals include: integral of sin(x) dx = -cos(x) + C, integral of cos(x) dx = sin(x) + C, integral of sec^2(x) dx = tan(x) + C, integral of csc^2(x) dx = -cot(x) + C, integral of e^x dx = e^x + C, integral of a^x dx = a^x/ln(a) + C. Example: Find integral of (e^x + 3*sin(x)) dx = e^x - 3*cos(x) + C.

title: Integration by Substitution (u-substitution)
formula_latex: \int f(g(x)) \cdot g'(x)\, dx = \int f(u)\, du \quad \text{where } u = g(x)
text: Integration by Substitution (u-substitution). If the integrand contains a composite function f(g(x))*g'(x), substitute u = g(x), du = g'(x) dx to simplify the integral to integral of f(u) du. After integration, substitute back u = g(x). This reverses the chain rule of differentiation. Example: Find integral of 2x*cos(x^2) dx. Let u = x^2, du = 2x dx. Integral becomes integral of cos(u) du = sin(u) + C = sin(x^2) + C.

title: Integration by Parts
text: Integration by Parts. Integration by parts formula: integral of u dv = uv - integral of v du. Choose u and dv using the LIATE rule (Logarithmic, Inverse trig, Algebraic, Trigonometric, Exponential). The function earlier in LIATE is typically chosen as u. Sometimes the method must be applied twice. Example: Find integral of x*e^x dx. Let u = x (algebraic), dv = e^x dx. Then du = dx, v = e^x. integral = x*e^x - integral of e^x dx = x*e^x - e^x + C = e^x(x - 1) + C.

title: Integration by Partial Fractions
text: Integration by Partial Fractions. A proper rational function P(x)/Q(x) (degree P < degree Q) can be decomposed into simpler fractions. For distinct linear factors: A/(x-a) + B/(x-b). For repeated factors: A/(x-a) + B/(x-a)^2. For irreducible quadratic factors: (Ax+B)/(x^2+px+q). Each simpler fraction is then integrated separately. Example: Find integral of 1/[(x-1)(x+2)] dx. Decompose: 1/[(x-1)(x+2)] = A/(x-1) + B/(x+2). Solving: A = 1/3, B = -1/3. Integral = (1/3)*ln|x-1| - (1/3)*ln|x+2| + C = (1/3)*ln|(x-1)/(x+2)| + C.

title: Trigonometric Substitution
formula_latex: \sqrt{a^2 - x^2}: x = a\sin\theta, \quad \sqrt{a^2 + x^2}: x = a\tan\theta, \quad \sqrt{x^2 - a^2}: x = a\sec\theta
text: Trigonometric Substitution. For integrands involving sqrt(a^2 - x^2), use x = a*sin(theta). For sqrt(a^2 + x^2), use x = a*tan(theta). For sqrt(x^2 - a^2), use x = a*sec(theta). These substitutions convert the square root into a trigonometric expression that simplifies the integral. Example: Find integral of 1/sqrt(4 - x^2) dx. Let x = 2*sin(theta), dx = 2*cos(theta) d(theta). sqrt(4 - 4sin^2(theta)) = 2*cos(theta). Integral = integral of 2*cos(theta)/(2*cos(theta)) d(theta) = theta + C = arcsin(x/2) + C.

title: Integration Using Special Forms
text: Integration Using Special Forms. Standard results for special forms: integral of dx/(x^2 + a^2) = (1/a)*arctan(x/a) + C. Integral of dx/(x^2 - a^2) = (1/2a)*ln|(x-a)/(x+a)| + C. Integral of dx/sqrt(x^2 + a^2) = ln|x + sqrt(x^2 + a^2)| + C. These are frequently tested in JEE. Example: Find integral of dx/(x^2 + 9). Here a^2 = 9, a = 3. Answer: (1/3)*arctan(x/3) + C.

title: Fundamental Theorem of Calculus
formula_latex: \int_a^b f(x)\, dx = F(b) - F(a) \quad \text{where } F'(x) = f(x)
text: Fundamental Theorem of Calculus. Part 1: If f is continuous on [a, b] and F(x) = integral from a to x of f(t) dt, then F'(x) = f(x). Part 2: If F is any antiderivative of f on [a, b], then integral from a to b of f(x) dx = F(b) - F(a). This theorem connects differentiation and integration. Example: Evaluate integral from 0 to pi of sin(x) dx. Antiderivative of sin(x) is -cos(x). = [-cos(x)] from 0 to pi = -cos(pi) - (-cos(0)) = -(-1) + 1 = 2.

title: Properties of Definite Integrals
formula_latex: \int_0^a f(x)\, dx = \int_0^a f(a-x)\, dx, \quad \int_{-a}^a f(x)\, dx = \begin{cases} 2\int_0^a f(x)\,dx & f \text{ even} \\ 0 & f \text{ odd} \end{cases}
text: Properties of Definite Integrals. Key properties: (1) integral from a to a of f(x) dx = 0. (2) integral from a to b = -integral from b to a. (3) integral from a to b = integral from a to c + integral from c to b for a < c < b. (4) integral from 0 to a of f(x) dx = integral from 0 to a of f(a-x) dx (King's property). (5) For even f: integral from -a to a = 2*integral from 0 to a. For odd f: integral from -a to a = 0. Example: Evaluate integral from 0 to pi/2 of sin(x)/(sin(x)+cos(x)) dx. Let I = the integral. By King's property: I = integral from 0 to pi/2 of cos(x)/(cos(x)+sin(x)) dx. Adding: 2I = integral from 0 to pi/2 of 1 dx = pi/2. So I = pi/4.

title: Area Under a Curve
formula_latex: A = \int_a^b |f(x)|\, dx, \quad A_{\text{between}} = \int_a^b [f(x) - g(x)]\, dx \text{ where } f(x) \geq g(x)
text: Area Under a Curve. The area bounded by y = f(x), the x-axis, and the lines x = a, x = b is given by the integral from a to b of |f(x)| dx. If f(x) >= 0 on [a, b], area = integral from a to b of f(x) dx. For area between two curves y = f(x) and y = g(x) where f >= g, area = integral from a to b of [f(x) - g(x)] dx. Example: Find the area enclosed between y = x^2 and y = x. Set x^2 = x, so x = 0 and x = 1. For 0 <= x <= 1, x >= x^2. Area = integral from 0 to 1 of (x - x^2) dx = [x^2/2 - x^3/3] from 0 to 1 = 1/2 - 1/3 = 1/6.

title: Walli's Formula and Reduction Formulas
formula_latex: \int_0^{\pi/2} \sin^n x\, dx = \frac{(n-1)!!}{n!!} \cdot \begin{cases} \frac{\pi}{2} & n \text{ even} \\ 1 & n \text{ odd} \end{cases}
text: Walli's Formula and Reduction Formulas. Walli's formula gives: integral from 0 to pi/2 of sin^n(x) dx = integral from 0 to pi/2 of cos^n(x) dx = [(n-1)/n]*[(n-3)/(n-2)]*...*[1/2]*(pi/2) for even n, and [(n-1)/n]*[(n-3)/(n-2)]*...*[2/3]*1 for odd n. These are useful for JEE problems involving powers of trig functions. Example: Find integral from 0 to pi/2 of sin^4(x) dx. n = 4 (even). = (3/4)*(1/2)*(pi/2) = 3*pi/16.

title: Order and Degree of Differential Equations
formula_latex: \text{Order: highest derivative order}, \quad \text{Degree: power of highest order derivative (polynomial form)}
text: Order and Degree of Differential Equations. The order of a differential equation (DE) is the order of the highest derivative present. The degree is the exponent of the highest order derivative after the DE is made free of radicals and fractions involving derivatives. The degree is defined only when the DE is a polynomial in its derivatives. Example: Find order and degree of (d^2y/dx^2)^3 + (dy/dx)^2 + sin(dy/dx) = 0. Order = 2 (highest derivative is d^2y/dx^2). Degree is not defined because of sin(dy/dx) - the equation is not polynomial in derivatives.

title: Variable Separable Method
text: Variable Separable Method. A first-order ODE dy/dx = f(x,y) is variable separable if it can be written as g(y) dy = h(x) dx. Integrate both sides separately: integral of g(y) dy = integral of h(x) dx + C. This is the simplest method for solving first-order ODEs. Example: Solve dy/dx = xy. Separate: dy/y = x dx. Integrate: ln|y| = x^2/2 + C. So y = A*e^(x^2/2) where A = e^C.

title: Linear Differential Equation (First Order)
formula_latex: \frac{dy}{dx} + P(x)y = Q(x), \quad \text{IF} = e^{\int P(x)\, dx}, \quad y \cdot \text{IF} = \int Q(x) \cdot \text{IF}\, dx + C
text: Linear Differential Equation (First Order). A first-order linear ODE has the form dy/dx + P(x)*y = Q(x). The solution is found using the integrating factor (IF) = e^(integral of P(x) dx). Multiply both sides by IF: d/dx[IF * y] = IF * Q(x). Then y * IF = integral of [IF * Q(x)] dx + C. Example: Solve dy/dx + y = e^x. P(x) = 1, IF = e^(integral of 1 dx) = e^x. Multiply: e^x*dy/dx + e^x*y = e^(2x). d/dx[y*e^x] = e^(2x). Integrate: y*e^x = e^(2x)/2 + C. So y = e^x/2 + C*e^(-x).

title: Homogeneous Differential Equations
formula_latex: \frac{dy}{dx} = F\left(\frac{y}{x}\right): \text{ let } y = vx \implies v + x\frac{dv}{dx} = F(v)
text: Homogeneous Differential Equations. A first-order ODE dy/dx = f(x,y) is homogeneous if f(tx, ty) = f(x,y) for all t, i.e., f depends only on y/x. Substitute y = vx, so dy/dx = v + x*dv/dx. This reduces the equation to a variable separable form in v and x. Example: Solve dy/dx = (x + y)/x = 1 + y/x. Let y = vx, dy/dx = v + x*dv/dx. So v + x*dv/dx = 1 + v, giving x*dv/dx = 1, dv = dx/x. Integrate: v = ln|x| + C. So y/x = ln|x| + C, i.e., y = x*ln|x| + Cx.

title: Concavity and Points of Inflection
formula_latex: f''(x) > 0 \implies \text{concave up}, \quad f''(x) < 0 \implies \text{concave down}, \quad \text{inflection at sign change of } f''
text: Concavity and Points of Inflection. A function is concave up (convex) where f''(x) > 0 and concave down where f''(x) < 0. A point of inflection is where concavity changes, i.e., f''(x) changes sign. At a point of inflection, f''(c) = 0 or f''(c) is undefined, but f''(x) = 0 alone is not sufficient. Example: Find inflection points of f(x) = x^3 - 3x^2 + 2. f'(x) = 3x^2 - 6x, f''(x) = 6x - 6 = 0 gives x = 1. f''(x) < 0 for x < 1 (concave down), f''(x) > 0 for x > 1 (concave up). Inflection at x = 1, f(1) = 0.

title: Leibniz Rule for Differentiation Under the Integral Sign
formula_latex: \frac{d}{dx}\int_{u(x)}^{v(x)} f(t)\, dt = f(v(x)) \cdot v'(x) - f(u(x)) \cdot u'(x)
text: Leibniz Rule for Differentiation Under the Integral Sign. If F(x) = integral from u(x) to v(x) of f(t) dt, then F'(x) = f(v(x))*v'(x) - f(u(x))*u'(x). When the limits are constants and f depends on x as a parameter, d/dx integral from a to b of f(x,t) dt = integral from a to b of partial f/partial x dt. This is a powerful technique for JEE advanced problems. Example: If F(x) = integral from 0 to x^2 of sin(t) dt, find F'(x). Here u(x) = 0, v(x) = x^2, f(t) = sin(t). F'(x) = sin(x^2) * 2x - sin(0) * 0 = 2x*sin(x^2).

title: Distance Formula
text: Distance Formula. The distance between two points (x1, y1) and (x2, y2) in a plane is given by sqrt((x2-x1)^2 + (y2-y1)^2). This is derived from the Pythagorean theorem applied to the horizontal and vertical displacements between the two points. Example: Distance between (1,2) and (4,6) = sqrt((4-1)^2 + (6-2)^2) = sqrt(9+16) = sqrt(25) = 5.

title: Section Formula
formula_latex: P = \left( \frac{m x_2 + n x_1}{m + n},\ \frac{m y_2 + n y_1}{m + n} \right)
text: Section Formula. The coordinates of the point dividing the line segment joining (x1, y1) and (x2, y2) in the ratio m:n internally are ((mx2 + nx1)/(m+n), (my2 + ny1)/(m+n)). For external division, replace + with - in the denominator and numerator signs accordingly: ((mx2 - nx1)/(m-n), (my2 - ny1)/(m-n)). Example: Point dividing the segment from (2,3) to (8,9) in the ratio 2:1 internally = ((2*8 + 1*2)/3, (2*9 + 1*3)/3) = (18/3, 21/3) = (6, 7).

title: Midpoint Formula
text: Midpoint Formula. The midpoint of a line segment joining two points (x1, y1) and (x2, y2) is the special case of the section formula where the ratio is 1:1. It gives the point that is equidistant from both endpoints. Example: Midpoint of segment joining (3, 5) and (7, 11) = ((3+7)/2, (5+11)/2) = (5, 8).

title: Area of a Triangle using Coordinates
formula_latex: \text{Area} = \frac{1}{2} \left| x_1(y_2 - y_3) + x_2(y_3 - y_1) + x_3(y_1 - y_2) \right|
text: Area of a Triangle using Coordinates. The area of a triangle formed by three vertices (x1, y1), (x2, y2), and (x3, y3) can be computed using the determinant method. The absolute value ensures a positive area regardless of the ordering of vertices. If the area equals zero, the three points are collinear. Example: Area of triangle with vertices (0,0), (4,0), (0,3) = (1/2)|0(0-3) + 4(3-0) + 0(0-0)| = (1/2)|0 + 12 + 0| = 6 sq. units.

title: Collinearity of Three Points
text: Collinearity of Three Points. Three points (x1, y1), (x2, y2), and (x3, y3) are collinear if and only if the area of the triangle formed by them is zero. Equivalently, the determinant formed by their coordinates equals zero. This is a fundamental test used in many JEE problems. Example: Check collinearity of (1,2), (3,6), (5,10): 1(6-10) + 3(10-2) + 5(2-6) = -4 + 24 - 20 = 0. Hence collinear.

title: Slope of a Line
text: Slope of a Line. The slope (m) of a line passing through two points (x1, y1) and (x2, y2) measures the steepness and direction of the line. A positive slope indicates the line rises from left to right, a negative slope indicates it falls. A horizontal line has slope 0 and a vertical line has undefined slope. The slope also equals tan(theta) where theta is the angle the line makes with the positive x-axis. Example: Slope of line through (2,3) and (5,9) = (9-3)/(5-2) = 6/3 = 2. The inclination angle = arctan(2) ≈ 63.43°.

title: Equations of a Line (Various Forms)
text: Equations of a Line (Various Forms). A straight line can be expressed in several standard forms: (1) Slope-intercept: y = mx + c, (2) Point-slope: y - y1 = m(x - x1), (3) Two-point form: (y-y1)/(y2-y1) = (x-x1)/(x2-x1), (4) Intercept form: x/a + y/b = 1, (5) Normal form: x cos(alpha) + y sin(alpha) = p, (6) General form: ax + by + c = 0. Choosing the right form simplifies problem-solving significantly in JEE. Example: Line with x-intercept 3 and y-intercept 4: x/3 + y/4 = 1, which simplifies to 4x + 3y = 12.

title: Angle Between Two Lines
text: Angle Between Two Lines. The acute angle between two lines with slopes m1 and m2 is found using the tangent formula. If the lines are perpendicular, m1*m2 = -1 (the denominator becomes zero and theta = 90°). If the lines are parallel, m1 = m2 and theta = 0°. This formula is extremely important for JEE problems involving bisectors and loci. Example: Angle between y = 2x + 1 (m1=2) and y = 3x - 1 (m2=3): tan(theta) = |2-3|/|1+6| = 1/7, so theta = arctan(1/7) ≈ 8.13°.

title: Distance from a Point to a Line
formula_latex: d = \frac{|a x_1 + b y_1 + c|}{\sqrt{a^2 + b^2}}
text: Distance from a Point to a Line. The perpendicular distance from a point (x1, y1) to the line ax + by + c = 0 is given by the formula below. This is used extensively in finding the distance between parallel lines (take any point on one line and find its distance to the other), radius of inscribed circles, and optimization problems. Example: Distance from (3, 4) to the line 3x + 4y - 5 = 0: d = |3(3) + 4(4) - 5| / sqrt(9+16) = |9+16-5|/5 = 20/5 = 4.

title: Family of Lines
formula_latex: (a_1 x + b_1 y + c_1) + \lambda (a_2 x + b_2 y + c_2) = 0
text: Family of Lines. The equation L1 + lambda*L2 = 0 represents the family of all lines passing through the point of intersection of lines L1 = 0 and L2 = 0, where lambda is a parameter. This concept is powerful for JEE problems because it avoids explicitly computing the intersection point. Any line through the intersection can be written in this form for some real value of lambda. Example: Family of lines through intersection of x + y - 1 = 0 and 2x - y + 3 = 0: (x+y-1) + lambda(2x-y+3) = 0. For the member passing through origin, put (0,0): (-1) + lambda(3) = 0, so lambda = 1/3.

title: General Equation of a Circle
formula_latex: x^2 + y^2 + 2gx + 2fy + c = 0, \quad r = \sqrt{g^2 + f^2 - c}
text: General Equation of a Circle. The general equation of a circle is x^2 + y^2 + 2gx + 2fy + c = 0, where the center is (-g, -f) and the radius is sqrt(g^2 + f^2 - c). For a valid circle, the condition g^2 + f^2 - c > 0 must hold. The standard form (x-h)^2 + (y-k)^2 = r^2 has center (h,k) and radius r. Example: Circle x^2 + y^2 - 6x + 4y - 12 = 0 has 2g=-6 so g=-3, 2f=4 so f=2. Center = (3,-2), radius = sqrt(9+4+12) = sqrt(25) = 5.

title: Tangent to a Circle
formula_latex: x x_1 + y y_1 = r^2 \quad \text{(tangent at } (x_1, y_1) \text{ on } x^2+y^2=r^2\text{)}
text: Tangent to a Circle. The equation of the tangent to the circle x^2 + y^2 = r^2 at a point (x1, y1) on the circle is xx1 + yy1 = r^2. For the general circle x^2 + y^2 + 2gx + 2fy + c = 0, the tangent at (x1,y1) is xx1 + yy1 + g(x+x1) + f(y+y1) + c = 0. A line y = mx + c is tangent to x^2 + y^2 = r^2 if c^2 = r^2(1 + m^2), known as the condition of tangency. Example: Tangent to x^2 + y^2 = 25 at (3,4): 3x + 4y = 25. Verify: slope = -3/4, and at (3,4): 9+16 = 25. Correct.

title: Normal to a Circle
text: Normal to a Circle. The normal to a circle at any point on it always passes through the center of the circle. For a circle with center (h, k), the normal at a point (x1, y1) on the circle is the line joining (x1, y1) and (h, k). This is because the radius is always perpendicular to the tangent at the point of tangency. Example: Normal to x^2 + y^2 = 25 at (3,4): the line passes through (3,4) and center (0,0), so slope = 4/3. Equation: y = (4/3)x.

title: Chord of Contact
formula_latex: T = 0 \implies x x_1 + y y_1 = r^2 \quad \text{(for } x^2 + y^2 = r^2\text{)}
text: Chord of Contact. The chord of contact is the line joining the two points of tangency drawn from an external point to a circle. From an external point (x1, y1), the chord of contact of the circle x^2 + y^2 = r^2 has the equation xx1 + yy1 = r^2 (same form as the tangent equation, but the point is external). This concept extends to conics as well and is a frequent JEE topic. Example: Chord of contact from (5, 5) to x^2 + y^2 = 16: 5x + 5y = 16, i.e., x + y = 16/5.

title: Family of Circles and Radical Axis
text: Family of Circles and Radical Axis. The family of circles passing through the intersection of circles S1 = 0 and S2 = 0 is S1 + lambda*S2 = 0 (lambda != -1). The radical axis of two circles S1 = 0 and S2 = 0 is the locus of points having equal power with respect to both circles, given by S1 - S2 = 0. The radical axis is always perpendicular to the line joining the centers. For three circles, the three radical axes are concurrent at the radical center. Example: Radical axis of x^2+y^2-4x-6=0 and x^2+y^2+2x-8=0: subtracting gives -4x-6-2x+8=0 => -6x+2=0 => x=1/3.

title: Standard Equation of a Parabola
text: Standard Equation of a Parabola. A parabola is the locus of a point equidistant from a fixed point (focus) and a fixed line (directrix). The standard form y^2 = 4ax has vertex at origin, focus at (a,0), directrix x = -a, axis along the x-axis, and latus rectum of length 4a. Other standard forms: y^2 = -4ax (opens left), x^2 = 4ay (opens up), x^2 = -4ay (opens down). Example: For y^2 = 12x: 4a = 12, so a = 3. Focus = (3,0), directrix: x = -3, latus rectum = 12.

title: Parametric Form and Tangent to a Parabola
text: Parametric Form and Tangent to a Parabola. For the parabola y^2 = 4ax, any point on it can be written parametrically as (at^2, 2at). The equation of the tangent at parameter t is ty = x + at^2. The tangent at a point (at1^2, 2at1) and at (at2^2, 2at2) intersect at the point (at1*t2, a(t1+t2)). The condition for a line y = mx + c to be tangent to y^2 = 4ax is c = a/m. Example: Tangent to y^2 = 8x at t=1: point = (2, 4). Tangent: 1*y = x + 2, i.e., y = x + 2. Verify: slope = 1, and y = mx + a/m = x + 2. Correct.

title: Focal Chord and Properties of a Parabola
text: Focal Chord and Properties of a Parabola. A focal chord of y^2 = 4ax is any chord passing through the focus (a, 0). If one end is at parameter t1, the other end is at t2 where t1*t2 = -1. The length of the focal chord at parameter t is a(t + 1/t)^2. The semi-latus rectum is the harmonic mean of the segments of any focal chord. Tangents drawn at the endpoints of a focal chord are perpendicular and meet on the directrix. Example: For y^2 = 4x (a=1), focal chord at t=2: other end at t=-1/2. Points: (4,4) and (1/4, -1). Length = a(t+1/t)^2 = 1*(2+0.5)^2 = 6.25.

title: Standard Equation of an Ellipse
text: Standard Equation of an Ellipse. An ellipse is the locus of a point whose sum of distances from two fixed points (foci) is constant (2a). The standard equation is x^2/a^2 + y^2/b^2 = 1 (a > b > 0), with center at origin, major axis along x-axis of length 2a, minor axis of length 2b. The relationship b^2 = a^2(1 - e^2) connects the semi-axes with eccentricity e. Foci are at (+-ae, 0) and directrices at x = +-a/e. Example: Ellipse x^2/25 + y^2/16 = 1: a=5, b=4, e = sqrt(1-16/25) = 3/5. Foci: (+-3, 0). Directrices: x = +-25/3.

title: Tangent to an Ellipse
formula_latex: \frac{x x_1}{a^2} + \frac{y y_1}{b^2} = 1 \quad \text{and} \quad c^2 = a^2 m^2 + b^2
text: Tangent to an Ellipse. The tangent to the ellipse x^2/a^2 + y^2/b^2 = 1 at a point (x1, y1) is xx1/a^2 + yy1/b^2 = 1. In parametric form, the point (a cos(theta), b sin(theta)) has tangent (x cos(theta))/a + (y sin(theta))/b = 1. A line y = mx + c is tangent to the ellipse if c^2 = a^2*m^2 + b^2 (condition of tangency). Example: Tangent to x^2/9 + y^2/4 = 1 at (3cos60°, 2sin60°) = (3/2, sqrt(3)): (x*(1/2))/3 + (y*(sqrt(3)/2))/2 = 1, i.e., x/6 + sqrt(3)y/4 = 1.

title: Properties of an Ellipse
formula_latex: \text{Latus Rectum} = \frac{2b^2}{a}, \quad \text{Director Circle: } x^2 + y^2 = a^2 + b^2
text: Properties of an Ellipse. Key properties: (1) Sum of focal distances for any point on the ellipse = 2a. (2) The product of perpendicular distances from the foci to any tangent equals b^2. (3) The latus rectum has length 2b^2/a. (4) The auxiliary circle has equation x^2 + y^2 = a^2. (5) Eccentric angle theta relates the ellipse to its auxiliary circle via (a cos(theta), b sin(theta)). (6) The director circle (locus of intersection of perpendicular tangents) is x^2 + y^2 = a^2 + b^2. Example: For x^2/25 + y^2/9 = 1: a=5, b=3. Latus rectum = 2(9)/5 = 18/5. Director circle: x^2 + y^2 = 34.

title: Standard Equation of a Hyperbola
text: Standard Equation of a Hyperbola. A hyperbola is the locus of a point whose absolute difference of distances from two foci is constant (2a). The standard equation is x^2/a^2 - y^2/b^2 = 1, with transverse axis along x-axis of length 2a and conjugate axis of length 2b. Here b^2 = a^2(e^2 - 1), eccentricity e > 1. Foci are at (+-ae, 0). The conjugate hyperbola is y^2/b^2 - x^2/a^2 = 1. Example: Hyperbola x^2/16 - y^2/9 = 1: a=4, b=3, e = sqrt(1+9/16) = 5/4. Foci: (+-5, 0).

title: Asymptotes of a Hyperbola
text: Asymptotes of a Hyperbola. The asymptotes of the hyperbola x^2/a^2 - y^2/b^2 = 1 are the lines y = (b/a)x and y = -(b/a)x. They pass through the center and are the diagonals of the rectangle formed by x = +-a and y = +-b. A rectangular hyperbola has a = b, giving perpendicular asymptotes y = +-x. The equation of asymptotes is x^2/a^2 - y^2/b^2 = 0. Importantly, the combined equation of the hyperbola, its asymptotes, and the conjugate hyperbola differ only in the constant term. Example: Asymptotes of x^2/9 - y^2/4 = 1: y = +-2x/3. These lines are approached by the hyperbola at infinity but never touched.

title: Tangent to a Hyperbola
formula_latex: \frac{x x_1}{a^2} - \frac{y y_1}{b^2} = 1, \quad c^2 = a^2 m^2 - b^2
text: Tangent to a Hyperbola. The tangent to the hyperbola x^2/a^2 - y^2/b^2 = 1 at a point (x1, y1) is xx1/a^2 - yy1/b^2 = 1. In parametric form using (a sec(theta), b tan(theta)), the tangent is (x sec(theta))/a - (y tan(theta))/b = 1. A line y = mx + c is tangent if c^2 = a^2*m^2 - b^2. The director circle (locus of perpendicular tangent intersection) is x^2 + y^2 = a^2 - b^2, which exists only when a > b. Example: Tangent to x^2/16 - y^2/9 = 1 with slope 2: c^2 = 16(4) - 9 = 55, so c = +-sqrt(55). Lines: y = 2x +- sqrt(55).

title: Rectangular Hyperbola (xy = c^2)
text: Rectangular Hyperbola (xy = c^2). The rectangular hyperbola xy = c^2 is a special form where the asymptotes are the coordinate axes. Any point on it can be written as (ct, c/t). The tangent at (ct, c/t) is x/t + yt = 2c. The normal at this point is xt^3 - yt - ct^4 + c = 0. The eccentricity of a rectangular hyperbola is always sqrt(2). This form appears frequently in JEE problems. Example: For xy = 4 (c=2), point at t=1: (2, 2). Tangent: x/1 + y(1) = 4, i.e., x + y = 4. Verify: at (2,2), 2+2=4. Correct.

title: Direction Cosines and Direction Ratios
formula_latex: l^2 + m^2 + n^2 = 1, \quad l = \frac{a}{\sqrt{a^2 + b^2 + c^2}}
text: Direction Cosines and Direction Ratios. Direction cosines (l, m, n) of a line are the cosines of the angles it makes with the positive x, y, and z axes respectively, satisfying l^2 + m^2 + n^2 = 1. Direction ratios (a, b, c) are any set of numbers proportional to the direction cosines: l = a/sqrt(a^2+b^2+c^2), etc. The direction ratios of a line joining points (x1,y1,z1) and (x2,y2,z2) are (x2-x1, y2-y1, z2-z1). Example: DR of line from (1,2,3) to (4,6,3) are (3,4,0). DCs: l = 3/5, m = 4/5, n = 0. Verify: 9/25 + 16/25 + 0 = 1.

title: Distance Between Two Points in 3D
text: Distance Between Two Points in 3D. The distance formula in three dimensions extends the 2D formula by including the z-coordinate term. It gives the length of the line segment joining two points in space. This is fundamental to all 3D geometry computations including finding the radius of a sphere, edge lengths, and verifying geometric shapes in space. Example: Distance from (1,2,3) to (4,6,3) = sqrt(9 + 16 + 0) = sqrt(25) = 5.

title: Equation of a Plane
formula_latex: ax + by + cz + d = 0, \quad d = \frac{|ax_1 + by_1 + cz_1 + d|}{\sqrt{a^2+b^2+c^2}}
text: Equation of a Plane. A plane in 3D can be expressed in several forms: (1) General form: ax + by + cz + d = 0, where (a,b,c) is the normal vector. (2) Normal form: lx + my + nz = p, where (l,m,n) are DCs of the normal and p is the perpendicular distance from origin. (3) Intercept form: x/a + y/b + z/c = 1. (4) Point-normal form: a(x-x1) + b(y-y1) + c(z-z1) = 0. The distance from point (x1,y1,z1) to plane ax+by+cz+d=0 is |ax1+by1+cz1+d|/sqrt(a^2+b^2+c^2). Example: Plane through (1,2,3) with normal (2,3,6): 2(x-1)+3(y-2)+6(z-3)=0 => 2x+3y+6z=26. Distance from origin = 26/sqrt(4+9+36) = 26/7.

title: Equation of a Line in 3D
text: Equation of a Line in 3D. A line in 3D can be expressed in: (1) Symmetric/Cartesian form: (x-x1)/a = (y-y1)/b = (z-z1)/c, passing through (x1,y1,z1) with direction ratios (a,b,c). (2) Vector form: r = a + lambda*b, where a is a position vector of a point on the line and b is the direction vector. (3) Two-point form: (x-x1)/(x2-x1) = (y-y1)/(y2-y1) = (z-z1)/(z2-z1). The angle between two lines is determined by the angle between their direction vectors. Example: Line through (1,2,3) with DRs (2,3,-1): (x-1)/2 = (y-2)/3 = (z-3)/(-1). At parameter t=1: point (3, 5, 2).

title: Angle Between a Line and a Plane, and Skew Lines Distance
text: Angle Between a Line and a Plane, and Skew Lines Distance. The angle theta between a line with DRs (a1,b1,c1) and a plane with normal (a2,b2,c2) satisfies sin(theta) = |a1*a2+b1*b2+c1*c2|/(sqrt(a1^2+b1^2+c1^2)*sqrt(a2^2+b2^2+c2^2)). The shortest distance between two skew lines r = a1 + lambda*b1 and r = a2 + mu*b2 is |(a2-a1).(b1 x b2)|/|b1 x b2|. Two lines are coplanar if this distance is zero. These concepts are critical for JEE Advanced. Example: Angle between line with DRs (1,1,1) and plane 2x+y-z=5 (normal (2,1,-1)): sin(theta) = |2+1-1|/(sqrt(3)*sqrt(6)) = 2/sqrt(18) = 2/(3*sqrt(2)), theta = arcsin(sqrt(2)/3).

title: Division Algorithm
text: Division Algorithm. For any integers a and b with b > 0, there exist unique integers q (quotient) and r (remainder) such that a = bq + r where 0 <= r < b. This is the foundation of divisibility theory and underpins the Euclidean algorithm. Example: 17 divided by 5: 17 = 5(3) + 2, so quotient = 3, remainder = 2.

title: Divisibility Rules for Common Numbers
formula_latex: n \mid a \iff a = nk \text{ for some integer } k
text: Divisibility Rules for Common Numbers. A number is divisible by 2 if its last digit is even; by 3 if the sum of its digits is divisible by 3; by 4 if the last two digits form a number divisible by 4; by 5 if it ends in 0 or 5; by 6 if divisible by both 2 and 3; by 8 if the last three digits form a number divisible by 8; by 9 if the sum of digits is divisible by 9; by 11 if the alternating sum of digits is divisible by 11. Example: Check if 2376 is divisible by 11: alternating sum = 2 - 3 + 7 - 6 = 0, and 11 | 0, so 11 | 2376.

title: Properties of Divisibility
formula_latex: a \mid b \text{ and } a \mid c \implies a \mid (bx + cy) \text{ for all integers } x, y
text: Properties of Divisibility. Key properties: (1) If a | b and a | c, then a | (b + c) and a | (b - c). (2) If a | b, then a | bc for any integer c. (3) If a | b and b | c, then a | c (transitivity). (4) If a | b and b | a, then a = +/- b. These properties are essential for proofs in number theory. Example: Since 6 | 18 and 6 | 24, we get 6 | (18 + 24) = 6 | 42, and indeed 42 = 6 * 7.

title: Euclidean Algorithm for GCD
text: Euclidean Algorithm for GCD. The Euclidean algorithm finds the GCD of two integers by repeatedly applying the division algorithm. Given a and b with a > b > 0, apply a = bq + r, then replace a with b and b with r, repeating until the remainder is 0. The last non-zero remainder is gcd(a, b). This is one of the oldest and most efficient algorithms in mathematics. Example: Find gcd(252, 105): 252 = 105(2) + 42; 105 = 42(2) + 21; 42 = 21(2) + 0. So gcd(252, 105) = 21.

title: Relationship Between GCD and LCM
text: Relationship Between GCD and LCM. For any two positive integers a and b, the product of their GCD and LCM equals the product of the numbers themselves. This fundamental relationship allows us to compute LCM efficiently once GCD is known. It extends to: lcm(a, b) = |a * b| / gcd(a, b). Example: For a = 12, b = 18: gcd(12, 18) = 6, so lcm(12, 18) = (12 * 18) / 6 = 216 / 6 = 36.

title: Bezout's Identity
text: Bezout's Identity. For any integers a and b (not both zero), there exist integers x and y such that ax + by = gcd(a, b). These coefficients x and y can be found using the Extended Euclidean Algorithm by back-substituting through the steps of the standard algorithm. This identity is crucial for solving linear Diophantine equations. Example: For a = 35, b = 15: gcd = 5. Using extended algorithm: 35(1) + 15(-2) = 35 - 30 = 5, so x = 1, y = -2.

title: Fundamental Theorem of Arithmetic
formula_latex: n = p_1^{a_1} \cdot p_2^{a_2} \cdot \ldots \cdot p_k^{a_k}, \quad p_1 < p_2 < \ldots < p_k
text: Fundamental Theorem of Arithmetic. Every integer greater than 1 can be represented uniquely as a product of prime numbers, up to the order of the factors. This is also known as the Unique Prime Factorization Theorem. It guarantees that prime factorization is a well-defined operation and forms the backbone of number theory. Example: 360 = 2^3 * 3^2 * 5^1. This factorization is unique (ignoring order of factors).

title: Tests for Primality
formula_latex: n \text{ is prime if } \nexists \, p \leq \sqrt{n} \text{ such that } p \mid n, \text{ where } p \text{ is prime}
text: Tests for Primality. To check if a number n is prime, it suffices to test divisibility by all primes up to the square root of n. If no prime p with p <= sqrt(n) divides n, then n is prime. This is because if n = a * b with a <= b, then a <= sqrt(n). This drastically reduces the number of checks needed. Example: Is 97 prime? sqrt(97) ~ 9.85, so check primes 2, 3, 5, 7. None divide 97, so 97 is prime.

title: Infinitude of Primes and Prime Counting
text: Infinitude of Primes and Prime Counting. There are infinitely many prime numbers (Euclid's theorem). The proof proceeds by contradiction: if p1, p2, ..., pn were all primes, then N = p1*p2*...*pn + 1 is not divisible by any pi, contradicting the assumption. The Prime Number Theorem states that the number of primes up to n, denoted pi(n), is approximately n / ln(n). Example: pi(100) = 25 (there are 25 primes up to 100). The approximation gives 100/ln(100) ~ 100/4.605 ~ 21.7.

title: Congruences and Basic Properties
text: Congruences and Basic Properties. We say a is congruent to b modulo m (written a ≡ b (mod m)) if m divides (a - b). Key properties: (1) Reflexive: a ≡ a (mod m). (2) Symmetric: a ≡ b implies b ≡ a. (3) Transitive: a ≡ b and b ≡ c implies a ≡ c. Congruences can be added, subtracted, and multiplied: if a ≡ b and c ≡ d (mod m), then a + c ≡ b + d and a*c ≡ b*d (mod m). Example: 17 ≡ 2 (mod 5) because 5 | (17 - 2) = 15. Also, 17 + 8 = 25 ≡ 2 + 3 = 5 ≡ 0 (mod 5).

title: Solving Linear Congruences
formula_latex: ax \equiv b \pmod{m} \text{ has solution } \iff \gcd(a, m) \mid b
text: Solving Linear Congruences. The linear congruence ax ≡ b (mod m) has a solution if and only if gcd(a, m) divides b. If d = gcd(a, m) divides b, the congruence has exactly d incongruent solutions modulo m. When gcd(a, m) = 1, there is a unique solution modulo m, found by computing the modular inverse of a. Example: Solve 3x ≡ 6 (mod 9): gcd(3, 9) = 3, and 3 | 6, so solutions exist. Dividing by 3: x ≡ 2 (mod 3). Solutions mod 9: x = 2, 5, 8.

title: Chinese Remainder Theorem
formula_latex: x \equiv a_i \pmod{m_i}, \quad i = 1, 2, \ldots, k \implies \text{unique } x \pmod{M}, \; M = \prod m_i
text: Chinese Remainder Theorem. If m1, m2, ..., mk are pairwise coprime positive integers, then the system of simultaneous congruences x ≡ a1 (mod m1), x ≡ a2 (mod m2), ..., x ≡ ak (mod mk) has a unique solution modulo M = m1*m2*...*mk. This theorem is widely used in JEE problems involving remainders and in cryptography. Example: Find x: x ≡ 2 (mod 3), x ≡ 3 (mod 5). M = 15. Testing: x = 8 gives 8 mod 3 = 2 and 8 mod 5 = 3. So x ≡ 8 (mod 15).

title: Euler's Totient Function
text: Euler's Totient Function. Euler's totient function phi(n) counts the number of integers from 1 to n that are coprime to n. For a prime p, phi(p) = p - 1. For a prime power, phi(p^k) = p^k - p^(k-1). For general n with prime factorization n = p1^a1 * p2^a2 * ... * pk^ak, the totient is computed using the product formula. This function is multiplicative: phi(ab) = phi(a)*phi(b) when gcd(a,b) = 1. Example: phi(12) = 12 * (1 - 1/2) * (1 - 1/3) = 12 * 1/2 * 2/3 = 4. The integers coprime to 12 in {1,...,12} are {1, 5, 7, 11}.

title: Euler's Theorem
text: Euler's Theorem. If a and n are coprime positive integers (gcd(a, n) = 1), then a raised to the power phi(n) is congruent to 1 modulo n. This generalizes Fermat's Little Theorem to composite moduli and is fundamental in RSA cryptography and competitive math for finding remainders of large powers. Example: Find the remainder of 7^222 mod 10. phi(10) = 4, gcd(7,10) = 1, so 7^4 ≡ 1 (mod 10). 222 = 4(55) + 2, so 7^222 ≡ 7^2 = 49 ≡ 9 (mod 10).

title: Fermat's Little Theorem
formula_latex: a^{p-1} \equiv 1 \pmod{p}, \quad \text{when } p \nmid a \text{ and } p \text{ is prime}
text: Fermat's Little Theorem. If p is a prime number and a is an integer not divisible by p, then a^(p-1) ≡ 1 (mod p). Equivalently, for any integer a, a^p ≡ a (mod p). This is a special case of Euler's theorem (since phi(p) = p - 1 for prime p) and is extensively used in JEE for computing remainders and in primality testing. Example: Find remainder of 2^100 mod 13. By Fermat: 2^12 ≡ 1 (mod 13). 100 = 12(8) + 4, so 2^100 ≡ 2^4 = 16 ≡ 3 (mod 13).

title: Principle of Mathematical Induction
formula_latex: P(n_0) \text{ true}, \quad P(k) \Rightarrow P(k+1) \quad \implies \quad P(n) \text{ true } \forall \, n \geq n_0
text: Principle of Mathematical Induction. Mathematical induction is a proof technique for statements P(n) defined for all natural numbers n >= n0. It consists of two steps: (1) Base case: Prove P(n0) is true. (2) Inductive step: Assume P(k) is true for some arbitrary k >= n0 (inductive hypothesis), then prove P(k+1) is true. If both steps are established, P(n) holds for all n >= n0. Example: Prove 1 + 2 + ... + n = n(n+1)/2. Base: n=1, LHS = 1, RHS = 1(2)/2 = 1. Inductive step: assume sum to k = k(k+1)/2, then sum to k+1 = k(k+1)/2 + (k+1) = (k+1)(k+2)/2.

title: Strong (Complete) Induction
formula_latex: P(n_0), \; \left[\forall j \; (n_0 \leq j \leq k) : P(j)\right] \Rightarrow P(k+1) \quad \implies \quad P(n) \; \forall \, n \geq n_0
text: Strong (Complete) Induction. Strong induction is a variant where the inductive hypothesis assumes P(n0), P(n0+1), ..., P(k) are all true (not just P(k)) and then proves P(k+1). Strong induction is logically equivalent to standard induction but is more natural for proofs where P(k+1) depends on multiple earlier cases, such as proving the Fundamental Theorem of Arithmetic. Example: Prove every integer n >= 2 is a product of primes. Base: 2 is prime. Inductive step: assume all integers from 2 to k are products of primes. If k+1 is prime, done. Otherwise k+1 = a*b with 2 <= a, b <= k, and by hypothesis both are products of primes, so k+1 is too.

title: Well-Ordering Principle
formula_latex: S \subseteq \mathbb{Z}^+, \; S \neq \emptyset \implies \exists \, m \in S : m \leq s \; \forall \, s \in S
text: Well-Ordering Principle. Every non-empty subset of the positive integers has a least element. This principle is logically equivalent to the Principle of Mathematical Induction and is often used in proofs by contradiction in number theory. To prove a property holds for all positive integers, assume the set of counterexamples is non-empty; by well-ordering it has a smallest element, and deriving a contradiction completes the proof. Example: Prove there is no positive integer between 0 and 1. Assume S = {n in Z+ : 0 < n < 1} is non-empty. By well-ordering, S has a least element m. Then m^2 < m and 0 < m^2, so m^2 is in S, contradicting m being least.

title: GCD and LCM via Prime Factorization
text: GCD and LCM via Prime Factorization.
