Given the prime factorizations of two numbers a and b, the GCD is obtained by taking the minimum exponent of each common prime factor, and the LCM is obtained by taking the maximum exponent of each prime factor appearing in either number. This method connects prime factorization to GCD/LCM computation directly. Example: a = 60 = 2^2 * 3 * 5, b = 45 = 3^2 * 5. gcd = 2^0 * 3^1 * 5^1 = 15, lcm = 2^2 * 3^2 * 5^1 = 180.

title: Wilson's Theorem
text: Wilson's Theorem. An integer p >= 2 is prime if and only if (p-1)! ≡ -1 (mod p). Equivalently, (p-1)! + 1 is divisible by p when p is prime. This theorem provides a necessary and sufficient condition for primality, though it is not computationally efficient for large numbers. It is frequently used in JEE/olympiad problems involving factorials and primes. Example: For p = 5: (5-1)! = 4! = 24. 24 mod 5 = 4 = 5 - 1 ≡ -1 (mod 5). Confirmed. For p = 4 (not prime): 3! = 6, 6 mod 4 = 2, which is not -1 mod 4.

title: Arithmetic Mean
text: Arithmetic Mean. The arithmetic mean of n observations x1, x2, ..., xn is their sum divided by n. For grouped data with class marks xi and frequencies fi, the mean equals the sum of fi*xi divided by the sum of fi. The assumed mean method and step-deviation method are useful shortcuts when data values are large. Example: Mean of 2, 4, 6, 8, 10 = (2+4+6+8+10)/5 = 30/5 = 6.

title: Median for Ungrouped and Grouped Data
text: Median for Ungrouped and Grouped Data. The median is the middle value when observations are arranged in ascending or descending order. For n observations: if n is odd, median = ((n+1)/2)th value; if n is even, median = average of (n/2)th and (n/2 + 1)th values. For grouped data, the median class is identified using cumulative frequency, then the exact median is interpolated using the median formula. Example: For the data 3, 5, 7, 9, 11 (n=5, odd), median = 3rd value = 7. For 3, 5, 7, 9 (n=4, even), median = (5+7)/2 = 6.

title: Mode for Ungrouped and Grouped Data
text: Mode for Ungrouped and Grouped Data. The mode is the value that occurs most frequently in a dataset. A dataset can be unimodal, bimodal, or multimodal. For grouped data, the modal class is the class with the highest frequency, and the exact mode is calculated using frequencies of the modal class and its neighbours. The empirical relation 3 Median = Mode + 2 Mean holds approximately for moderately skewed distributions. Example: For the data 2, 3, 3, 5, 7, 7, 7, 9, the mode is 7 since it appears 3 times, more than any other value.

title: Range and Mean Deviation
text: Range and Mean Deviation. Range is the simplest measure of dispersion: Range = Maximum value - Minimum value. Mean deviation about a central value a (mean or median) is the average of absolute deviations from a. Mean deviation about the median is always less than or equal to mean deviation about any other value. For grouped data, multiply each absolute deviation by its frequency before averaging. Example: For data 6, 7, 10, 12, 13, 4, 8, 12: mean = 9. Mean deviation = (|6-9|+|7-9|+|10-9|+|12-9|+|13-9|+|4-9|+|8-9|+|12-9|)/8 = (3+2+1+3+4+5+1+3)/8 = 22/8 = 2.75.

title: Variance and Standard Deviation
text: Variance and Standard Deviation. Variance is the mean of the squares of deviations from the arithmetic mean. Standard deviation is the positive square root of variance. A shortcut formula avoids computing the mean first: variance = (mean of squares) - (square of mean). For grouped data, replace sums with frequency-weighted sums. Standard deviation has the same unit as the data, making it more interpretable than variance. Example: For data 4, 6, 8: mean = 6. Variance = ((4-6)^2 + (6-6)^2 + (8-6)^2)/3 = (4+0+4)/3 = 8/3. Standard deviation = sqrt(8/3) ~ 1.633.

title: Coefficient of Variation
text: Coefficient of Variation. The coefficient of variation (CV) is a dimensionless measure that expresses standard deviation as a percentage of the mean. It is used to compare the variability of two datasets that have different units or different means. A higher CV indicates greater relative variability. The dataset with a smaller CV is considered more consistent or stable. Example: Dataset A: mean = 50, SD = 5, CV = (5/50)*100 = 10%. Dataset B: mean = 100, SD = 8, CV = (8/100)*100 = 8%. Dataset B is more consistent since its CV is lower.

title: Classical Definition of Probability
formula_latex: P(A) = \frac{\text{Number of favourable outcomes}}{\text{Total number of outcomes}} = \frac{m}{n}, \quad 0 \le P(A) \le 1
text: Classical Definition of Probability. If a random experiment has n equally likely, mutually exclusive, and exhaustive outcomes, and m of these are favourable to event A, then P(A) = m/n. Probability always lies between 0 and 1, inclusive. P(sure event) = 1 and P(impossible event) = 0. The complement of event A is A', and P(A') = 1 - P(A). Example: When rolling a fair die, P(getting a number greater than 4) = favourable outcomes {5, 6} / total outcomes {1,2,3,4,5,6} = 2/6 = 1/3.

title: Addition Theorem of Probability
formula_latex: P(A \cup B) = P(A) + P(B) - P(A \cap B)
text: Addition Theorem of Probability. For any two events A and B, the probability that at least one occurs is given by the addition theorem. For mutually exclusive events (A and B cannot occur simultaneously), P(A intersection B) = 0, so the formula simplifies to P(A union B) = P(A) + P(B). This extends to three events with inclusion-exclusion. Example: A card is drawn from a standard deck. P(King) = 4/52, P(Heart) = 13/52, P(King and Heart) = 1/52. P(King or Heart) = 4/52 + 13/52 - 1/52 = 16/52 = 4/13.

title: Conditional Probability
text: Conditional Probability. The conditional probability of event A given that event B has already occurred is denoted P(A|B). It restricts the sample space to outcomes in B. Two events are independent if P(A|B) = P(A), equivalently P(A intersection B) = P(A)*P(B). The multiplication rule P(A intersection B) = P(B)*P(A|B) follows directly from the definition. Example: Two cards are drawn without replacement. P(2nd is Ace | 1st is Ace) = 3/51, since after removing one ace, 3 aces remain among 51 cards.

title: Theorem of Total Probability
text: Theorem of Total Probability. If B1, B2, ..., Bn form a partition of the sample space (mutually exclusive and exhaustive events), then the probability of any event A can be expressed as a weighted sum using the law of total probability. This is fundamental to Bayes' theorem and is used when the probability of A depends on which partition event has occurred. Example: A factory has 3 machines producing 30%, 45%, 25% of output with defect rates 2%, 3%, 1%. P(defective) = 0.30*0.02 + 0.45*0.03 + 0.25*0.01 = 0.006 + 0.0135 + 0.0025 = 0.022 or 2.2%.

title: Bayes' Theorem
text: Bayes' Theorem. Bayes' theorem provides a way to update the probability of a hypothesis given new evidence. Given a partition B1, B2, ..., Bn of the sample space, the posterior probability P(Bi|A) is computed using the prior probabilities P(Bi) and likelihoods P(A|Bi). It reverses the conditioning: from P(A|Bi) to P(Bi|A). Widely used in medical testing, spam filtering, and decision-making. Example: Using the factory example: P(Machine 2 | defective) = (0.45*0.03)/0.022 = 0.0135/0.022 = 0.6136 or about 61.4%.

title: Applications of Bayes' Theorem
text: Applications of Bayes' Theorem. Bayes' theorem is commonly applied to medical diagnosis problems (sensitivity and specificity of tests), quality control in manufacturing, and decision problems under uncertainty. The key steps are: (1) identify the partition events (hypotheses), (2) list priors P(Bi), (3) list likelihoods P(A|Bi), (4) compute total probability P(A), (5) apply Bayes' formula for the desired posterior. Example: A disease affects 1% of a population. A test has 99% sensitivity and 95% specificity. P(Disease|Positive) = (0.99*0.01)/((0.99*0.01)+(0.05*0.99)) = 0.0099/0.0594 = 0.1667 or about 16.7%.

title: Discrete Random Variables and Probability Distribution
text: Discrete Random Variables and Probability Distribution. A random variable X is a real-valued function defined on a sample space. A discrete random variable takes countable values x1, x2, ..., xn with respective probabilities p1, p2, ..., pn. The probability distribution (or PMF) satisfies: pi >= 0 for all i, and the sum of all pi equals 1. The cumulative distribution function F(x) = P(X <= x) is a non-decreasing step function. Example: For a fair coin tossed twice, let X = number of heads. P(X=0) = 1/4, P(X=1) = 2/4 = 1/2, P(X=2) = 1/4. Verify: 1/4 + 1/2 + 1/4 = 1.

title: Continuous Random Variables
formula_latex: P(a \le X \le b) = \int_a^b f(x)\, dx, \quad \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} f(x)\, dx = 1
text: Continuous Random Variables. A continuous random variable can take any value in an interval. Its probability distribution is described by a probability density function (PDF) f(x) where f(x) >= 0 and the total area under the curve equals 1. The probability that X lies in [a, b] is the integral of f(x) from a to b. For a continuous RV, P(X = c) = 0 for any specific value c. Example: If f(x) = 2x for 0 <= x <= 1 and 0 otherwise, then P(0.5 <= X <= 1) = integral from 0.5 to 1 of 2x dx = [x^2] from 0.5 to 1 = 1 - 0.25 = 0.75.

title: Expectation (Mean) of a Random Variable
formula_latex: E(X) = \sum_{i=1}^{n} x_i \cdot P(X = x_i), \quad E(aX + b) = aE(X) + b
text: Expectation (Mean) of a Random Variable. The mathematical expectation or expected value E(X) is the long-run average value of a random variable. For discrete RVs, it is the sum of each value times its probability. Key properties: E(aX + b) = aE(X) + b (linearity), E(X + Y) = E(X) + E(Y) for any X, Y. Expected value need not be a value that X can actually take. Example: For a fair die, E(X) = 1*(1/6) + 2*(1/6) + 3*(1/6) + 4*(1/6) + 5*(1/6) + 6*(1/6) = 21/6 = 3.5.

title: Variance of a Random Variable
text: Variance of a Random Variable. The variance Var(X) measures how spread out the values of X are around the mean. It equals the expected value of the squared deviation from the mean. The shortcut formula Var(X) = E(X^2) - [E(X)]^2 is computationally convenient. Key properties: Var(aX + b) = a^2 Var(X), Var(X) >= 0, and Var(constant) = 0. Example: For a fair die: E(X) = 3.5, E(X^2) = (1+4+9+16+25+36)/6 = 91/6. Var(X) = 91/6 - (3.5)^2 = 91/6 - 12.25 = 2.9167.

title: Fundamental Principle of Counting and nPr
text: Fundamental Principle of Counting and nPr. The multiplication principle states: if one task can be done in m ways and a second task in n ways, both together can be done in m*n ways. A permutation is an arrangement of objects where order matters. The number of permutations of r objects chosen from n distinct objects is nPr = n!/(n-r)!. Special case: n! is the number of permutations of all n objects. Example: Number of 3-letter arrangements from {A, B, C, D, E} = 5P3 = 5!/(5-3)! = 120/2 = 60.

title: Permutations with Repetition and Identical Objects
text: Permutations with Repetition and Identical Objects. When r objects are chosen from n types with unlimited repetition, the number of arrangements is n^r. When permuting n objects where p1 are of type 1, p2 of type 2, ..., pk of type k, the number of distinct permutations is n!/(p1! * p2! * ... * pk!). This handles problems like arranging letters in a word with repeated letters. Example: Arrangements of the letters in MISSISSIPPI: 11 letters with M=1, I=4, S=4, P=2. Answer = 11!/(1!*4!*4!*2!) = 39916800/1152 = 34650.

title: Circular Permutations
text: Circular Permutations. In a circular arrangement, one position is fixed to avoid counting rotations of the same arrangement. The number of ways to arrange n distinct objects in a circle is (n-1)!. If clockwise and anticlockwise arrangements are considered identical (as in a necklace or bracelet), the count is (n-1)!/2. Example: 8 people sit around a circular table: arrangements = (8-1)! = 7! = 5040. If the table has no distinguishable orientation (like a rotating platform), it remains 5040 since people are distinguishable. For 8 beads on a bracelet: (8-1)!/2 = 5040/2 = 2520.

title: Combinations (nCr) and Properties
text: Combinations (nCr) and Properties. A combination is a selection of objects where order does not matter. The number of ways to choose r objects from n distinct objects is nCr = n!/(r!(n-r)!). Key properties: nC0 = nCn = 1, nCr = nC(n-r) (symmetry), nCr + nC(r-1) = (n+1)Cr (Pascal's identity). Combinations are the building blocks of the binomial theorem. Example: Choosing a committee of 3 from 7 people = 7C3 = 7!/(3!*4!) = 5040/(6*24) = 35.

title: Applications of Combinations
formula_latex: \text{Diagonals of n-gon} = \binom{n}{2} - n = \frac{n(n-3)}{2}, \quad \text{Triangles from n points} = \binom{n}{3}
text: Applications of Combinations. Combinations are used in problems involving selection of teams, distributing objects, choosing subsets, and counting paths in grids. Common problem types: selecting r items from n with constraints (at least, at most, exactly), distributing identical objects into distinct groups (stars and bars), and counting diagonals or triangles from polygon vertices. Example: Number of diagonals of an octagon (n=8) = 8C2 - 8 = 28 - 8 = 20. Number of triangles from 10 non-collinear points = 10C3 = 120.

title: Bernoulli Trials and Binomial Distribution
formula_latex: P(X = r) = \binom{n}{r} p^r q^{n-r}, \quad r = 0, 1, 2, \ldots, n, \quad q = 1 - p
text: Bernoulli Trials and Binomial Distribution. A Bernoulli trial is an experiment with exactly two outcomes: success (probability p) and failure (probability q = 1 - p). The binomial distribution models the number of successes X in n independent Bernoulli trials. X can take values 0, 1, 2, ..., n. It is denoted X ~ B(n, p). Conditions: fixed number of trials, independent trials, constant probability of success, and two outcomes per trial. Example: A fair coin is tossed 5 times. P(exactly 3 heads) = 5C3 * (1/2)^3 * (1/2)^2 = 10 * 1/8 * 1/4 = 10/32 = 5/16.

title: Mean and Variance of Binomial Distribution
text: Mean and Variance of Binomial Distribution. For X ~ B(n, p), the mean (expected value) is np and the variance is npq where q = 1 - p. The standard deviation is sqrt(npq). The mean is always greater than or equal to the variance since q <= 1. The distribution is symmetric when p = 0.5, right-skewed when p < 0.5, and left-skewed when p > 0.5. Example: A biased coin with P(head) = 0.6 is tossed 20 times. Mean = 20*0.6 = 12 heads. Variance = 20*0.6*0.4 = 4.8. SD = sqrt(4.8) = 2.19.

title: Binomial Distribution - Advanced Problems
text: Binomial Distribution - Advanced Problems. Advanced binomial problems involve finding the most likely value (mode), computing cumulative probabilities P(X >= k) or P(X <= k), and using the recurrence relation P(X=r+1)/P(X=r) = (n-r)p/((r+1)q) to build the distribution efficiently. The mode of B(n,p) lies at floor((n+1)p) or (n+1)p - 1 when (n+1)p is an integer. JEE problems often involve finding n and p given mean and variance. Example: If mean = 6 and variance = 4 for a binomial RV, then np = 6 and npq = 4, so q = 4/6 = 2/3, p = 1/3, n = 18. Mode = floor(19 * 1/3) = floor(6.33) = 6.

title: Combined Mean and Variance of Two Groups
text: Combined Mean and Variance of Two Groups. When two groups of sizes n1 and n2 with means x1_bar, x2_bar and variances s1^2, s2^2 are combined, the combined mean and variance can be computed without going back to individual observations. This is useful in problems where statistics of subgroups are known and the overall statistics are required. The combined variance accounts for both within-group and between-group variability. Example: Group A: n1=30, mean=50, var=16. Group B: n2=20, mean=60, var=25. Combined mean = (30*50+20*60)/50 = 2700/50 = 54. d1=50-54=-4, d2=60-54=6. Combined variance = (30*(16+16)+20*(25+36))/50 = (960+1220)/50 = 2180/50 = 43.6.

title: Pythagorean Identity
text: Pythagorean Identity. sin^2(x) + cos^2(x) = 1 for all real x. This is the fundamental trigonometric identity derived from the unit circle definition. Every point (cos x, sin x) lies on the unit circle x^2 + y^2 = 1. Example: If sin(x) = 3/5, then cos^2(x) = 1 - 9/25 = 16/25, so cos(x) = +/-4/5.

title: Secondary Pythagorean Identities
text: Secondary Pythagorean Identities. Dividing sin^2(x) + cos^2(x) = 1 by cos^2(x) gives 1 + tan^2(x) = sec^2(x). Dividing by sin^2(x) gives 1 + cot^2(x) = csc^2(x). These are essential for simplifying expressions involving tan, sec, cot, and csc. Example: If tan(x) = 7/24, then sec^2(x) = 1 + 49/576 = 625/576, so sec(x) = +/-25/24.

title: Reciprocal Identities
formula_latex: \csc x = \frac{1}{\sin x}, \quad \sec x = \frac{1}{\cos x}, \quad \cot x = \frac{1}{\tan x}
text: Reciprocal Identities. The six trigonometric functions form three reciprocal pairs: csc(x) = 1/sin(x), sec(x) = 1/cos(x), and cot(x) = 1/tan(x). These hold wherever the denominator is non-zero. Example: If sin(x) = 1/2, then csc(x) = 2. If cos(x) = sqrt(3)/2, then sec(x) = 2/sqrt(3) = 2*sqrt(3)/3.

title: Quotient Identities
formula_latex: \tan x = \frac{\sin x}{\cos x}, \quad \cot x = \frac{\cos x}{\sin x}
text: Quotient Identities. tan(x) = sin(x)/cos(x) and cot(x) = cos(x)/sin(x). These relate the tangent and cotangent functions to sine and cosine, and are used extensively to convert expressions into a single function type for simplification. Example: If sin(x) = 5/13 and cos(x) = 12/13, then tan(x) = (5/13)/(12/13) = 5/12.

title: Sine of Sum and Difference
text: Sine of Sum and Difference. sin(A + B) = sin A cos B + cos A sin B and sin(A - B) = sin A cos B - cos A sin B. These compound angle formulas are foundational and used to derive double-angle, half-angle, and product formulas. Example: sin(75) = sin(45 + 30) = sin45*cos30 + cos45*sin30 = (sqrt(2)/2)(sqrt(3)/2) + (sqrt(2)/2)(1/2) = (sqrt(6) + sqrt(2))/4.

title: Cosine of Sum and Difference
text: Cosine of Sum and Difference. cos(A + B) = cos A cos B - sin A sin B and cos(A - B) = cos A cos B + sin A sin B. Note the sign reversal compared to the sine formula: the sum formula has a minus sign. Example: cos(15) = cos(45 - 30) = cos45*cos30 + sin45*sin30 = (sqrt(2)/2)(sqrt(3)/2) + (sqrt(2)/2)(1/2) = (sqrt(6) + sqrt(2))/4.

title: Tangent of Sum and Difference
text: Tangent of Sum and Difference. tan(A + B) = (tan A + tan B)/(1 - tan A tan B) and tan(A - B) = (tan A - tan B)/(1 + tan A tan B). These are undefined when the denominator equals zero, which occurs when A + B or A - B equals an odd multiple of pi/2. Example: tan(75) = tan(45 + 30) = (1 + 1/sqrt(3))/(1 - 1/sqrt(3)) = (sqrt(3) + 1)/(sqrt(3) - 1) = 2 + sqrt(3).

title: Double Angle Formula for Sine
text: Double Angle Formula for Sine. sin(2A) = 2 sin A cos A. This is derived by setting B = A in the sin(A + B) formula. It is widely used in integration, solving equations, and simplifying expressions. Example: If A = 30, then sin(60) = 2*sin(30)*cos(30) = 2*(1/2)*(sqrt(3)/2) = sqrt(3)/2.

title: Double Angle Formulas for Cosine
text: Double Angle Formulas for Cosine. cos(2A) has three equivalent forms: cos^2 A - sin^2 A, 2cos^2 A - 1, and 1 - 2sin^2 A. The choice of form depends on the context. The last two are especially useful for deriving half-angle formulas and power-reduction identities. Example: cos(60) = 2cos^2(30) - 1 = 2*(3/4) - 1 = 1/2. Alternatively, cos(60) = 1 - 2sin^2(30) = 1 - 2*(1/4) = 1/2.

title: Double Angle Formula for Tangent
text: Double Angle Formula for Tangent. tan(2A) = 2 tan A / (1 - tan^2 A). This follows from setting B = A in the tan(A + B) formula. It is undefined when tan^2 A = 1, i.e., A = pi/4 + n*pi/2. Example: If tan(A) = 1/2, then tan(2A) = 2*(1/2)/(1 - 1/4) = 1/(3/4) = 4/3.

title: Half Angle Formulas for Sine and Cosine
text: Half Angle Formulas for Sine and Cosine. sin(A/2) = +/-sqrt((1 - cos A)/2) and cos(A/2) = +/-sqrt((1 + cos A)/2). The sign depends on the quadrant in which A/2 lies. These are derived from the double-angle formulas for cosine by replacing A with A/2. Example: sin(15) = sin(30/2) = sqrt((1 - cos30)/2) = sqrt((1 - sqrt(3)/2)/2) = sqrt((2 - sqrt(3))/4) = sqrt(2 - sqrt(3))/2.

title: Half Angle Formula for Tangent
text: Half Angle Formula for Tangent. tan(A/2) = sin A / (1 + cos A) = (1 - cos A) / sin A. An alternate form uses the substitution t = tan(A/2), giving sin A = 2t/(1+t^2) and cos A = (1-t^2)/(1+t^2). This t-substitution (Weierstrass substitution) is powerful for integration and solving equations. Example: tan(22.5) = tan(45/2) = sin45/(1 + cos45) = (sqrt(2)/2)/(1 + sqrt(2)/2) = sqrt(2)/(2 + sqrt(2)) = sqrt(2) - 1.

title: Product-to-Sum Formulas
text: Product-to-Sum Formulas. These convert products of sines and cosines into sums: 2 sin A cos B = sin(A+B) + sin(A-B), 2 cos A cos B = cos(A-B) + cos(A+B), 2 sin A sin B = cos(A-B) - cos(A+B). They are derived directly from the compound angle formulas by addition and subtraction. Example: 2 sin(50) cos(10) = sin(60) + sin(40) = sqrt(3)/2 + sin(40).

title: Sum-to-Product Formulas
text: Sum-to-Product Formulas. These convert sums/differences of sines and cosines into products: sin C + sin D = 2 sin((C+D)/2) cos((C-D)/2), sin C - sin D = 2 cos((C+D)/2) sin((C-D)/2), cos C + cos D = 2 cos((C+D)/2) cos((C-D)/2), cos C - cos D = -2 sin((C+D)/2) sin((C-D)/2). These are especially useful for solving trigonometric equations. Example: sin(50) + sin(10) = 2 sin(30) cos(20) = 2*(1/2)*cos(20) = cos(20).

title: General Solution for sin(x) = sin(alpha)
text: General Solution for sin(x) = sin(alpha). The general solution of sin(x) = sin(alpha) is x = n*pi + (-1)^n * alpha, where n is any integer. This captures both the principal solution and all co-terminal and supplementary angle solutions on the sine curve. Example: Solve sin(x) = 1/2. Here alpha = pi/6. General solution: x = n*pi + (-1)^n * pi/6. For n=0: x=pi/6, n=1: x=5pi/6, n=2: x=13pi/6, etc.

title: General Solution for cos(x) = cos(alpha)
text: General Solution for cos(x) = cos(alpha). The general solution of cos(x) = cos(alpha) is x = 2n*pi +/- alpha, where n is any integer. This accounts for the even symmetry of the cosine function. Example: Solve cos(x) = 1/2. Here alpha = pi/3. General solution: x = 2n*pi +/- pi/3. This gives x = ..., -5pi/3, -pi/3, pi/3, 5pi/3, 7pi/3, ...

title: General Solution for tan(x) = tan(alpha)
text: General Solution for tan(x) = tan(alpha). The general solution of tan(x) = tan(alpha) is x = n*pi + alpha, where n is any integer. The tangent function has period pi and no symmetry ambiguity, making this the simplest general solution form. Example: Solve tan(x) = 1. Here alpha = pi/4. General solution: x = n*pi + pi/4, giving x = ..., -3pi/4, pi/4, 5pi/4, 9pi/4, ...

title: Equations Reducible to Quadratics
formula_latex: a\sin^2 x + b\sin x + c = 0 \implies \text{let } t = \sin x
text: Equations Reducible to Quadratics. Many trigonometric equations can be converted into quadratic form using substitution (e.g., let t = sin x or t = cos x) or by using identities like cos(2x) = 1 - 2sin^2(x). After solving for t, back-substitute and apply the appropriate general solution formula. Always check that solutions satisfy the original domain constraints. Example: Solve 2sin^2(x) - 3sin(x) + 1 = 0. Let t = sin(x): 2t^2 - 3t + 1 = 0 gives (2t-1)(t-1) = 0, so t = 1/2 or t = 1. Thus x = n*pi + (-1)^n*(pi/6) or x = (4n+1)*pi/2.

title: Domain and Range of Inverse Trigonometric Functions
text: Domain and Range of Inverse Trigonometric Functions. The principal value branches are: arcsin: [-1,1] -> [-pi/2, pi/2], arccos: [-1,1] -> [0, pi], arctan: R -> (-pi/2, pi/2), arccot: R -> (0, pi), arcsec: |x|>=1 -> [0, pi]\{pi/2}, arccsc: |x|>=1 -> [-pi/2, pi/2]\{0}. These restricted ranges ensure the inverse functions are well-defined (one-to-one). Example: arcsin(1/2) = pi/6 (not 5pi/6, which is outside [-pi/2, pi/2]). arccos(-1/2) = 2pi/3 (not -2pi/3, which is outside [0, pi]).

title: Complementary Inverse Trigonometric Identities
text: Complementary Inverse Trigonometric Identities. arcsin(x) + arccos(x) = pi/2 for all x in [-1,1]. Similarly, arctan(x) + arccot(x) = pi/2 for all real x, and arcsec(x) + arccsc(x) = pi/2 for |x| >= 1. These reflect the co-function relationships sin and cos, tan and cot, sec and csc. Example: If arcsin(3/5) = alpha, then arccos(3/5) = pi/2 - alpha. Numerically, arcsin(0.6) approx 0.6435 and arccos(0.6) approx 0.9273, and their sum is pi/2 approx 1.5708.

title: Addition Formulas for Inverse Tangent
text: Addition Formulas for Inverse Tangent. arctan(a) + arctan(b) = arctan((a+b)/(1-ab)) when ab < 1, arctan(a) + arctan(b) = pi + arctan((a+b)/(1-ab)) when ab > 1 and a > 0, and arctan(a) + arctan(b) = -pi + arctan((a+b)/(1-ab)) when ab > 1 and a < 0. This is crucial in JEE for simplifying sums of inverse tangents. Example: arctan(1/2) + arctan(1/3) = arctan((1/2 + 1/3)/(1 - 1/6)) = arctan((5/6)/(5/6)) = arctan(1) = pi/4.

title: Inverse Trigonometric Functions of Negative Arguments
text: Inverse Trigonometric Functions of Negative Arguments. arcsin(-x) = -arcsin(x) (odd function), arccos(-x) = pi - arccos(x), arctan(-x) = -arctan(x) (odd function), arccot(-x) = pi - arccot(x). These symmetry properties are essential for evaluating inverse trig at negative arguments. Example: arcsin(-sqrt(3)/2) = -arcsin(sqrt(3)/2) = -pi/3. arccos(-1/2) = pi - arccos(1/2) = pi - pi/3 = 2pi/3.

title: Sine Rule (Law of Sines)
text: Sine Rule (Law of Sines). In any triangle ABC with sides a, b, c opposite to angles A, B, C respectively: a/sin A = b/sin B = c/sin C = 2R, where R is the circumradius. This is used when two angles and a side are known, or two sides and a non-included angle are given (ambiguous case). Example: In triangle ABC, A = 30, B = 45, a = 10. Then b = a*sin(B)/sin(A) = 10*sin(45)/sin(30) = 10*(sqrt(2)/2)/(1/2) = 10*sqrt(2).

title: Cosine Rule (Law of Cosines)
text: Cosine Rule (Law of Cosines). In any triangle ABC: a^2 = b^2 + c^2 - 2bc*cos(A), and similarly for b^2 and c^2 by cyclic permutation. This generalizes the Pythagorean theorem (when A = 90, the formula reduces to a^2 = b^2 + c^2). It is used when three sides are known (SSS) or two sides and the included angle (SAS). Example: In triangle ABC, b = 5, c = 7, A = 60. Then a^2 = 25 + 49 - 2(5)(7)cos(60) = 74 - 70*(1/2) = 74 - 35 = 39, so a = sqrt(39).

title: Area of a Triangle Using Trigonometry
text: Area of a Triangle Using Trigonometry. The area of triangle ABC = (1/2)*a*b*sin(C) = (1/2)*b*c*sin(A) = (1/2)*a*c*sin(B). This is derived from the standard base-height formula by expressing the height in terms of a sine. It is especially useful when two sides and the included angle are known. Example: In triangle with sides a = 8, b = 6, and included angle C = 30. Area = (1/2)(8)(6)sin(30) = 24*(1/2) = 12 square units.

title: Heights and Distances: Angle of Elevation and Depression
text: Heights and Distances: Angle of Elevation and Depression. The angle of elevation is the angle between the horizontal and the line of sight looking upward at an object. The angle of depression is the angle below the horizontal when looking downward. By alternate interior angles, the angle of depression from point A to point B equals the angle of elevation from B to A. These problems typically use tan(theta) = opposite/adjacent in right triangles. Example: A tower is observed from a point 100m away. The angle of elevation to the top is 60. Height of tower = 100*tan(60) = 100*sqrt(3) approx 173.2 m.

title: Maximum and Minimum of a*sin(x) + b*cos(x)
text: Maximum and Minimum of a*sin(x) + b*cos(x). The expression a*sin(x) + b*cos(x) can be written as R*sin(x + phi) where R = sqrt(a^2 + b^2) and tan(phi) = b/a. Therefore its maximum value is sqrt(a^2 + b^2) and minimum value is -sqrt(a^2 + b^2). This technique is frequently tested in JEE. Example: Find the maximum of 3sin(x) + 4cos(x). R = sqrt(9+16) = 5. Maximum value = 5, achieved when sin(x + phi) = 1 where phi = arctan(4/3).

title: Triple Angle Formulas
text: Triple Angle Formulas. sin(3A) = 3sin(A) - 4sin^3(A) and cos(3A) = 4cos^3(A) - 3cos(A). Also tan(3A) = (3tan(A) - tan^3(A))/(1 - 3tan^2(A)). These are derived by writing 3A = 2A + A and applying compound and double angle formulas. They appear frequently in JEE Advanced problems. Example: sin(3*30) = sin(90) = 1. Verify: 3sin(30) - 4sin^3(30) = 3*(1/2) - 4*(1/8) = 3/2 - 1/2 = 1. Correct.
